experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donate button on mobile impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 10/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button for mobile users. We hypothesized that showing the donation amount at the point of conversion would reduce friction by reinforcing the user’s selection and thus would increase conversion rate.
We had run this test earlier in the year with different results. For this experiment, we ran it on a Gaza-specific donation form that was getting traffic due to recent news in the area.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test assessed whether showing the donation amount on the "Donate" button increased mobile donor confidence and completion rates.
Key Results:
The conversion lift was statistically significant — the overall conversion rate increased by 37% at the 99% confidence level. Revenue Per Visitor (RPV) also trended upward by 12%; however, this change was not statistically significant, as it did not reach the 95% confidence threshold.
What the Results Suggest:
Displaying the amount on the button helps mobile donors commit with more confidence.

experiments.title: How defaulting a donation module to monthly giving impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to monthly for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2025 - 08/13/2025
Throughout the year, we tested a donation module as a replacement for a hero image and saw increases in conversions across the board. The next iteration of this test was to determine whether defaulting to monthly donations affected conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Defaulting to monthly donations led to a significant increase in conversions for potential donors.

2. The treatment group saw a 9.7% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.5%).

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. Consider defaulting to monthly donations in future donation modules to potentially improve conversion rates.

2. Continue to experiment with different variables such as donation amounts, payment methods, and messaging to further optimize conversion rates.

3. Monitor and analyze the results of future experiments to continuously improve the donation process and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a impact statement below the donate button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement below the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting the potential impact of their gift at the donate button would act as an extra layer of reinforcement and encourage the user to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2025
CaringBridge had a 2 day match. For this match, we wanted to test adding an impact statement below the donate button that highlighted that their gift would be doubled during this match. The statement read as follows –
Your $x gift goes twice as far, keeping this a free platform for patients, caregivers, and families to receive support and coordinate care every single day throughout a difficult health journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact statement below the donate button did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant 21.5% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.7% confidence level.

In future experimentation, it's important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of impact statements or any additional content on donation pages. It's possible that the impact statement used in this experiment was not compelling enough or may have caused confusion or distraction for potential donors.

There was a decrease in revenue as well. There was an 18% decrease in revenue with a 41% level of confidence

Control - $17,443

Treatment - $14,520

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different variations of impact statements, adjust the placement of the statement on the page, or even consider other strategies to emphasize the impact of donations. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors through surveys or qualitative research could provide valuable insights into what resonates with them and what motivates them to donate.

Overall, while this experiment did not yield the desired results, it provides valuable insights that can inform and improve future fundraising strategies for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How adding value prop to the donate button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop to the donate button on Care Net’s main donation page will achieve more donations because it will encourage users to click the donate button. It will remind them why they are giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2025 - 12/08/2025
Care Net’s donation pages typically have a donate button that says “Give Now” under that it says “Amount: $XX.” We were interested in seeing what would happen if we replaced the “Amount: $XX” with additional value prop. We replaced it with “I want to protect life.” We ran this experiment on their main donation page because that’s where they see the majority of their traffic and we knew this test would have a better chance of validating.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment showed that adding a value-based statement to the donate button (replacing “Amount: $XX” with “I want to protect life”) improved donation performance on Care Net’s highest-traffic donation page. The treatment produced a 44% lift in conversion rate (from 18.7% to 27.0%) with 99.3% confidence, indicating a statistically valid result. The key learning is that donors responded more strongly to mission/identity language than to transactional pricing cues, suggesting that reinforcing the “why” at the moment of decision can increase giving behavior.

experiments.title: How a more modernized donation form design impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more modern design for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2025 - 11/21/2025
Save the Children US has been using the same donation form design for a number of years, so we wanted to test whether a more modern look and improved visibility of key features would increase conversion rates by making the experience more appealing and accessible to potential donors. We ran this experiment on their evergreen donation form so as to not impact any campaign-specific results.
Instead of testing every single element to get to a ‘best practices’ form, we decided to test a major redesign first, and then in subsequent tests look at how changes to each element of the form impacted conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redesigning the main donation form. We hypothesized that a modernized design, combined with improved visibility of the form, would increase conversion by making the experience more appealing and accessible to donors.
Key Results:
-       47% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       200% statistically significant increase (100%) in overall RPV.
The results show a directional increase in overall conversion and RPV, suggesting that the improved design and visibility of the donation form encouraged more users to complete a donation.
Overall results:
Control: 25% conversion rate
Treatment: 37% conversion rate, which is a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased dramatically, with a 200% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Desktop versus mobile:
Since the biggest design changes were on desktop (the mobile version of the new form stacked similarly to the control) we expected the biggest changes on desktop:
Desktop:
Control: 27% conversion rate
Treatment: 41% conversion rate, which is a 48% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 225% with a 99% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 21% conversion rate
Treatment: 26% conversion rate, which was a 22% increase with only a 43% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor on mobile also increased slightly, with an 8% increase, but only a 56% level of confidence.
One-time versus recurring:
Recurring:
Control: 5.9% conversion rate
Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 28% decrease with a 56% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased, however, by 91% with a 76% level of confidence (the donation sample size, however, was low, with a larger gift skewing this metric).
One-time:
Control: 19.5% conversion rate.
Treatment: 33% conversion rate, which is a 70% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 211% with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Testing Form Friction: Will Requiring a Phone Number on a Content Offer Landing Page Hurt Sign-Ups from Facebook Traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that requiring a mobile phone number on the sign-up form for a Facebook video series acquisition offer will result in a small drop in conversions but more valuable contacts because motivated users will tolerate the added friction in exchange for high-value content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2025 - 11/11/2025
Focus on the Family has been working to grow their SMS program and wanted to test the impact of requiring a mobile phone number on a content sign-up form.
We decided to test this on a free video series titled Contagious Joy, which was being promoted on Facebook with acquisition ads. Visitors who clicked the ad would land on a sign-up page with a form asking for First Name, Last Name, and Email Address.
In the Treatment version of the page, a fourth required field—Mobile Phone Number—was added. All other elements of the page remained the same.
The test was set up as a split URL experiment, with 50% of traffic directed to the control version and 50% to the treatment.
This test aimed to answer a key question: How much friction can we introduce in the name of better data collection—without meaningfully hurting conversion rates?
We did expect a potential drop in sign-ups due to the added field but hypothesized that the value of acquiring phone numbers would outweigh the slight loss in volume.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results we saw were surprising. We had anticipated a slight drop in email conversions, but didn’t expect it to be as significant as it was. Requiring a phone number led to a 26.4% decrease in email sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence—clear evidence that this added field created too much friction for users coming from Facebook ads.
We also saw an almost statistically significant 40% drop in transactions on the post-sign-up donation page (92.2% LOC), suggesting the impact extended beyond the initial form. While average gift amount and recurring donor conversion didn’t change, the decrease in overall donor volume is still meaningful.
For this acquisition audience, the added requirement of a phone number appeared to outweigh the perceived value of the free video series. This test shows that asking for too much, too soon—especially from new visitors—can hurt both lead generation and potential revenue.
Going forward, it may be more effective to collect phone numbers later in the journey such as on the donation page, once trust has been built or value has been delivered. Or test adding the Phone Number field on the sign-up page but do not make it required to try and reduce friction.

experiments.title: Does concise donation form copy outperform long-form messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we shorten and clarify the donation form copy to present a concise value proposition, then more donors will complete their gifts (conversion rate increase) and potentially give at higher levels (revenue increase). This is because WUNC’s multichannel donors are already primed and don’t need long explanations—clear, streamlined copy will reduce friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 10/06/2025
This experiment was conducted on WUNC’s primary donation form, which receives the majority of donor traffic from email, direct navigation, and on-site promotions. The goal was to evaluate whether shorter, more action-oriented copy would improve donor conversion rates compared to WUNC’s traditional, longer-form messaging.
WUNC’s standard donation form historically emphasized emotional storytelling, a sense of community, and shared responsibility for supporting public radio in North Carolina. However, recent research in donation behavior and user experience has suggested that minimizing friction—especially in digital donation flows—can lead to higher conversion rates. Internally, there was interest in exploring whether this applied to WUNC’s audience, especially during high-engagement periods.
The team hypothesized that short, punchy copy emphasizing urgency and clarity might increase conversion by reducing cognitive load and decision-making time. However, there was concern that cutting too much might sacrifice the warmth, trust, and shared identity that WUNC’s donors often cite as core motivations for giving.

experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
Focus on the Family (FOTF) uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.
At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.
The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.
Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a high urgency campaign impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a monthly first ask for Fall Share giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving. because  more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2025 - 09/26/2025
Each year, Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign to inspire generosity among listeners.
This year, we made a strategic shift: instead of prioritizing one-time gifts, we focused on growing Moody’s recurring donor file. To accomplish this, we tested a monthly-first ask during the Fall Share campaign.
The goal was to build their recurring file while minimizing the negative impact on overall conversion on the giving page.
The test ran for 11 days, with traffic evenly split between each variant.
Note: The majority of participants came from known audiences. While a separate non-donor–specific donation page was available, many of the web assets directed to this general campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a monthly-first ask for Fall Share donation page visitors significantly increased recurring giving. The treatment group experienced a 168.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence in the results.
Furthermore, with this large increase in recurring giving, we wanted to be sure it wasn't at the expense of overall transactions. After further analysis, we discovered the treatment was resulting in a 5.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 42% level of confidence. Based on these results, the value of the more recurring donors outweighs the slight (and invalid) decrease in overall giving.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize the monthly-first ask strategy, as it proved to be highly effective in increasing recurring donor conversions without significantly reducing overall transactions. This approach capitalizes on the convenience and appeal of monthly giving for donors, leading to a higher likelihood of acceptance of the pre-set option and a perception of monthly giving as both the recommended and more manageable choice. By consistently incorporating this strategy in future campaigns and experiments, we can continue to grow our recurring donor file and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How defaulting donation form to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors to recurring will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it reduces decision fatigue and makes it feel like the expected choice, encouraging donors to follow through with the default option.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2025 - 06/27/2025
In an effort to generate more recurring donors for IMB, we opted to default their Bible Direct Ask donation form to recurring transactions for 50% of traffic.
The only change made on the treatment was the form default.
This test ran for about 2 weeks.
We anticipated seeing some level of increase in recurring gifts, but our goal was to also not see a large drop off in overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are: Defaulting the donation page to recurring for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donations by 647.9% at a valid 99.8% level of confidence. These results indicate that defaulting to recurring on a donation form will, in fact, lead to more recurring gifts on this funnel.

We did see a 14.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 53% LOC, but ultimately, a recurring donor is much more valuable than an episodic donor; therefore, the overall trade-off is worth the cost.

We kept the copy and value proposition consistent between the control and treatment to test how the form itself impacted recurring gifts, but in future experimentation, we plan to test using recurring language on the donation page along with a recurring defaulted form.

experiments.title: How adding PayPal as a giving option on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of PayPal for Pro-Life Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics  because  it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering an additional way to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 05/28/2025
For ACI’s Pro-Life Pledge donation page, we tested the integration of PayPal, leveraging Anedot’s native PayPal feature. Our goal was to assess whether adding PayPal would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding PayPal as a payment option to the Pro-Life Donation Page resulted in a notable 28% increase in transactions across all traffic. This outcome was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.5%, providing a solid foundation for future decision-making.
While we saw a general uptick in donations, we wanted to determine whether the increase was due to the availability of more payment options or if PayPal itself was driving the change.
Upon reviewing the data, we found that 30% of the donations on the test page were processed through PayPal. This suggests that PayPal is a trusted and credible giving method for ACI’s audience. Additionally, the donation lift closely aligns with the percentage of PayPal transactions, further supporting the impact of this payment option.
We plan to test this strategy in another ACI funnel to verify the results, and based on these findings, we’ll decide whether to implement PayPal across all ACI donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding Express Checkout options to a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for pledge donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering additional ways to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/13/2025
We wanted to test the addition of Express Checkout options, leveraging native Express Checkout features. Our goal was to assess whether these payment options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment suggest that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for the pledge donation page did not have the intended positive impact on donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in transactions at 36.6% compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 99.9%.

Specifically, the one-time donation option saw a substantial 384% decrease in transactions within the treatment group. This indicates that offering Express Checkout options may have introduced friction into the donation process for visitors, leading to a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully evaluate the impact of introducing new payment options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or surveys before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, analyzing donor preferences and behavior patterns can help in making informed decisions about optimizing the donation process.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of understanding the needs and preferences of donors when implementing changes to donation processes.

Additionally, it could be valuable to explore other elements of the sign-up process or website design to continue optimizing for user engagement and conversion. Conducting more experiments with different variables and monitoring the results will help in making informed decisions for improving sign-up rates on the website.

experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the email sign up form to the course registration page will increase the email acquisition because it reduces the friction at the decision point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2025 - 05/23/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test on the DTS Revelation Free Online Course registration page. The audience: People from social media ads.
We want to know if more people will sign up for the course if the form is embedded on the register page. The original version a person had to click a button to go to the sign up form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the email sign-up form directly to the course registration page significantly increased email acquisition for Dallas Theological Seminary. The treatment group experienced a 307.4% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the change had a substantial impact.

In future experimentation, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider simplifying the registration process by reducing the steps required for users to sign up. This can help reduce friction at the decision point and potentially lead to more conversions. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of the registration process, such as the placement of the form or the wording of the call-to-action, can provide valuable insights into what resonates best with the target audience.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the user experience to make it as seamless as possible for individuals to engage with the organization's offerings, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates and increased participation in their educational programs.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Living with Contagious Joy, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for the donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/10/2025
To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for the mission.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a treatment page, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 17 days and tested the impact of defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page within the “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel.

While the treatment successfully increased recurring transactions by 313.8% with a 98.2% level of confidence, it also led to a 45.2% decrease in one-time donations, resulting in an overall 33% drop in total transactions. This indicates that although more donors opted for monthly giving when it was pre-selected, the change significantly discouraged one-time donations.

Given these results, the test highlights a valuable trade-off: optimizing for recurring giving can improve long-term donor value, but may reduce total donor acquisition in the short term. Future testing could explore ways to elevate the visibility and value of monthly giving —such as using stronger messaging, layout hierarchy, or design emphasis. Additionally, tracking the retention and lifetime value of these newly acquired monthly donors will be critical to understanding if the short-term loss in total transactions is outweighed by long-term donor value.

experiments.title: How an “Disagree” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Disagree” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/15/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Agree” as opposed to a blank value or “Undecided” would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Disagree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to take action by answering survey questions and in turn increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Disagree” or “No” as the default.
The experiment ran for two weeks and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting to "Disagree" in dropdown lists for survey answers led to a significant 77.3% decrease in email acquisitions. This result suggests that the assumption that setting a negative default option would encourage action was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it's essential to carefully consider the impact of default options in survey questions. It is clear from this experiment that defaulting to a negative answer might discourage participants from engaging or taking action, resulting in a decrease in desired outcomes such as email acquisitions. Therefore, when designing future experiments, it would be beneficial to test different default options or even consider changing the dropdown lists back to radio buttons to see which approach yields the best results.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and hypotheses rigorously before implementing changes. It is crucial to analyze the potential impact of any alterations to the survey design thoroughly and be prepared to pivot based on the actual outcomes observed.

experiments.summary:

The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Undecided” as opposed to a blank value would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease of 32%.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Agree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to submit the survey and increase email acquisitions.

This indicates that simply defaulting to a positive response like "Agree" may not necessarily encourage survey takers to submit the survey or increase email acquisitions. It is essential to carefully consider all aspects of the survey design and wording, as well as understanding the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different variables and elements of the survey , including testing different wording for the default dropdown list answer, adjusting the layout or design of the survey, or even exploring other formats such as open text responses.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of thorough testing and the need to adapt strategies based on data-driven insights rather than assumptions.

experiments.title: How form location on a survey landing page impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the form below the survey for MPR Listener Survey landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because participants will focus solely on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront, creating a more seamless and user-friendly experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/20/2025
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on MPR’s Listener Survey landing page, we opted to test into relocating the form to the bottom of the page.
Our hypothesis was that this change would reduce the friction happening at the top of the page therefore increasing the number of people who would complete the survey.
Control: form above the survey
Treatment: form below the survey
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the form below the survey on the MPR's Listener Survey landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. The treatment version, where the form was placed at the bottom of the page, saw a 39.2% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic with a 99.1% statistical level of confidence, indicating that this change was highly effective in improving the user experience and increasing conversions and should be rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of page layout on user behavior. Placing the form below the survey reduced the friction at the top of the page, allowing participants to focus on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront. This created a more seamless and user-friendly experience ultimately leading to a higher conversion rate.

Moving forward, we should continue to test different page layout elements, such as form placement, language, and design, to optimize the user experience and improve conversion rates. It is also important to analyze the results of each experiment carefully and draw actionable insights to inform future optimization strategies.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 03/14/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $25 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 233 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on Seeing Beautiful Again, one of FamilyLife’s best performing offers.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this retest, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option once again led to an increase in recurring donations. The Treatment group, which featured recurring giving as the default, generated 16 recurring gifts compared to 0 in the Control group. This resulted in a 0.92% recurring conversion rate, validated by a 99.9% level of confidence—providing strong evidence that this approach effectively encourages more donors to commit to ongoing support.
When looking at immediate revenue, there was little difference between the two groups. The Control group generated $902 in revenue, while the Treatment brought in $1,017—a modest increase that is not statistically significant. However, while short-term revenue remained stable, the shift toward recurring gifts has the potential to yield much greater long-term value.
Although overall transactions were slightly lower in the Treatment group (93 gifts) compared to the Control (105 gifts), this was observed at a 66.8% level of confidence. This outcome is to be expected, as introducing a default recurring gift option may cause some one-time donors to reconsider their gift in the moment. However, those who do proceed are more likely to become committed, long-term supporters.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Continuing to test and optimize recurring donation strategies: Based on the success of this experiment, it is essential to explore and refine different approaches to promoting recurring gifts. Testing variations in messaging, default options, and impact statements can help further increase recurring donations.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/05/2025
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
This experiment was previously tested from October 16 – November 12, with the original test defaulting to a $15 recurring gift. The results showed that defaulting to recurring significantly increased recurring donations—the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. Based on these results, we initially moved forward with defaulting other pages to recurring gifts. However, as we continued monitoring performance, we observed lower ROIs and encountered limitations in the donation form’s gift array, which restricted customization for one-time and monthly gift amounts. As a result, we ultimately reverted the default back to a one-time gift.
Towards the end of the year, we saw a noticeable increase in sustainer giving, prompting us to revisit this test.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
Control: Defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The gift array included “$30, $50, $100, and $200,” with no pre-selected amount. The copy specifically asked for $30, while $50 was highlighted as a “popular” gift option. Note: No changes were made to the current live pages for Proof You Need for the control group.
Treatment: Defaulted to a recurring gift, with a gift array of “$15, $30, $50, and $100,” with $30 pre-selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 17 more recurring transactions than the control, resulting in an 830.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 100% confidence level.
However, overall transactions decreased slightly, with the treatment group converting 122 donations versus 141 in the control group. Revenue for the control was $5,520, while the treatment generated $4,470. Despite this initial decline in immediate revenue, the additional 17 recurring donors in the treatment group, with an average gift of $30 and an 85% retention rate, project to generate an additional $5,202 in revenue over time—demonstrating the long-term value of sustaining donors.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While immediate revenue per visitor declined, this result aligns with previous findings that indicate the long-term value of recurring donors outweighs short-term losses.
Next Steps
Default to Recurring Gifts for This Offer: Given the strong lift in recurring transactions and projected long-term revenue, we will permanently default this offer to recurring gifts.Test on Another Offer (Living with Contagious Joy): To further validate these findings, we will run a similar test on another offer, "Living with Contagious Joy," to assess whether the impact holds across different campaigns.Refine Messaging: Continue optimizing the way we present the recurring gift option to further encourage commitment while minimizing any deterrent effect on one-time donations.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track retention and lifetime value of these new recurring donors to ensure the strategy continues to drive sustainable growth for Focus on the Family.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a survey affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
because  the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2024 - 08/23/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a survey on Voter Fraud where people can take the survey and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight fraud in elections. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on Voter Fraud survey donation pages include the following:
1. Decreasing gift array amounts can increase donor conversion rate: The treatment, which presented lower gift amounts of $10, $25, and $50, saw an 18.0% increase in donations, with a valid 98% level of confidence, compared to the control with the $25, $50, and $100 amounts. This indicates that lowering the suggested gift amounts made donating feel more attainable to a larger number of people, leading to a higher conversion rate.
2. Increase in one-time transactions: The treatment group also showed a 16.8% increase in one-time donations at a valid 97% level of confidence, demonstrating that the lower gift array amounts not only attracted more donors, but also encouraged more one-time contributions.
3. Decrease in average gift amount: The treatment group also led to a 14.8% decrease in average gift, indicating that lower gift amounts may lead to people giving lower amounts, but at a 4% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.
Additional testing may include lower gift amounts on other survey pages as well as other types of offers or direct appeals.

Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.

The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. However, overall transactions decreased by 15.1%, with the treatment group converting 157 donations versus 187 in the control group. Additionally, revenue per visitor dropped by 22.4%, but this result only reached a 5.3% level of confidence, indicating that the difference is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While the average gift size was slightly lower in the treatment group ($31.16 vs. $33.76), recurring donors provide more sustainable support over time. The long-term value of recurring donations, as seen in similar experiments, outweighs the short-term decrease in revenue.

On this page, the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 13 conversions compared to just 3 in the control group, marking a 348.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. This result mirrors the impact seen in the "Proof You Need" test, reinforcing the effectiveness of defaulting to recurring gifts.
However, overall transactions decreased in the treatment group, with 67 conversions compared to 89 in the control group, resulting in a 22.1% decrease in conversion rate at an 89% confidence level. Revenue per visitor also dropped by 50.4%, from $2.37 to $1.50 per visitor. This result, though significant, had a low confidence level of 1.28%, suggesting that the drop in revenue is not as pronounced as the figures suggest. The average gift size also decreased in the treatment group ($27.99 vs. $34.42), which aligns with the expected shift toward smaller, recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in overall transactions and average gift size, the surge in recurring donations points to the long-term value of building a recurring donor base. The increase in recurring donors highlights a sustainable revenue stream that will contribute to Focus on the Family’s mission over time, even though immediate revenue was lower.
We believe that defaulting to recurring gifts, along with messaging that emphasizes their broader impact, made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The resulting increase in recurring donations suggests that the strategy can help Focus on the Family build stronger, longer-term relationships with donors.
Next Steps:

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/29/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $15 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on 5 premium donation pages, including two best-performing offers: Seeing Beautiful Again and 52 Weeks in the Word.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift resulted in 20 conversions compared to 0 in the Control group, marking a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 100% level of confidence. Revenue was down by 29.4%, but this was at a 16.1% level of confidence, indicating that the change is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue. Overall transactions were lower, reflecting the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. However, we identified that FamilyLife's recurring donors have a significantly higher retention rate of 77% compared to 24% for episodic donors, an increase in retention of 247%, underscoring the value of focusing on recurring contributions for the organization's long-term sustainability.
The increase in recurring donations can be attributed to the effective use of a default recurring gift option, which likely made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The enhanced messaging, which emphasized the broader impact of recurring gifts, probably resonated more with donors and encouraged them to choose the recurring option.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Refine Impact Messaging: Continue testing variations of impact messaging to determine the most effective approach for driving recurring donations.Optimize Recurring Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to find the most appealing option for donors.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How using a ‘reverse funnel’ design that places the form above the fold affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an email acquisition form above the fold will increase sign-up rates by capitalizing on the momentum created by earlier steps in the funnel and giving the user the option to read more if they choose.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
If we’re being honest, we don’t think a prospect should have to read several paragraphs of copy to see the value in exchanging their email address for a content offer. It’s generally accepted that 8 out of 10 people will read your headline/subheadline and nothing else, which means your “above-the-fold” copy is, without a doubt, far and away, the most important copy on the page.
Knowing this and working forward from the assumption that someone had to land on our landing page from somewhere — presumably some form of marketing communication that has already, at least, teased the offer …
We wanted to test what would happen if we seized the existing funnel momentum to drive the conversion by placing the form above the fold (directly beneath the headline and subheadline) and moving the ‘explainer’ copy down the page where a user could scroll to read if they so desired.
The treatment kept the headline and subhead copy unchanged and simply moved the body copy ‘below the fold’ where people could continue reading if they wanted to know more about the offer.
**Important note: although they do not show up in the renders below, both the control and the treatment featured 3D renders of the asset being promoted in the upper righthand space of the page
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the form above the fold resulted in a statistically valid 56.3% lift in email acquisition rates.
By giving people the option to 'fast-track' the download process, we were able to see more conversions. Importantly, this test is in no way meant to suggest that 'less copy is better.' We did not remove the copy; we simply moved it down the page so that people could forego additional reading if they were already 'sold.'
What this test does show is that your headline and subhead should be strong enough to carry the conversion on their own and that placing the form above the fold may create a more frictionless experience for enough users to make a significant difference in conversion rates.
It also shows the importance of analyzing each asset on its own and as part of the broader funnel. Avoid repeating the same message in your ad, email, landing page, donation, page, etc. Consider the momentum your other awesome assets have already created and capitalize on that to drive the conversion!
In future experimentation, we will focus on testing the headline, subhead, button copy, and any other above-the-fold elements, as this test shows that 'above-the-fold optimization' likely has an outsized impact on conversion rates

experiments.title: How adding a callout to the monthly tab on the donation form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout for for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it draws attention to the monthly option and adds value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/14/2024
In an effort to boost donor conversion metrics on MSLF’s Searle Petition, we decided to test including a callout on the monthly tab of the form. We have tested this concept with other MSLF offers and seen positive results.
The callout was only shown to half of the traffic and it read: “By giving a monthly gift, you are not just supporting our legal team’s defense 365 days a year, but you are safeguarding your Constitutional rights, your family’s Constitutional rights, and the rights of countless others.”
We believe that adding a callout for recurring giving for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it draws the visitor’s attention to the monthly option as well as provides additional value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly callout for potential donors actually decreased recurring donations by 79.3% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 97.1% confidence level, indicating a strong likelihood that the decrease was not due to random chance.

After reviewing other donor conversion metrics, we saw some very interesting results. For one-time gifts, we actually saw a 70.7% lift in donations at an 87.5% LOC. While we did not anticipate that a monthly callout would result in an increase in one-time gifts, it does suggest that there is a psychological concept that we could tap into.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further explore donor motivations and preferences through qualitative research or surveys. Understanding donors' perspectives and how they perceive different messages can provide valuable insights for designing effective fundraising strategies. Additionally, continuing to test different variations and closely monitoring the results will help in optimizing donation forms for increased conversions in the future.

experiments.title: How does a button on a slide-out impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating the form directly into the slideout will streamline the process, reducing friction and the number of steps required, potentially resulting in a higher number of sign-ups. Conversely, a slideout without a form may decrease the perceived commitment, thereby encouraging more sign-ups after users have gained additional information on the landing page
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
In an effort to enhance email acquisitions for a content offer, an experiment was conducted for WUNC, a non-profit news radio station. This experiment was tested on the homepage of their website to determine whether prospects are more likely to sign up for an offer directly within a slideout or by being redirected to a landing page. The aim is to gain insights into the most effective strategy for maximizing email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which included a link to direct users to a separate landing page, resulted in a 56.5% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to an embedded sign-up form.
2. By requiring users to navigate to a separate landing page to provide their information, the treatment potentially decreased the perceived commitment, leading to more sign-ups. This suggests that after users have gained additional information on the landing page, they are more likely to sign up for a content offer
3. The results of the experiment showed a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the increase in email acquisitions was not due to chance.
The key learnings from this experiment can guide decision-making in future experiments. It is recommended to continue testing different sign-up processes to further optimize email acquisitions. Additionally, user convenience and reducing perceived commitment can be important factors to consider when designing and implementing sign-up forms on websites.

experiments.title: How using monthly giving language and defaulting to the recurring tab in the donation form affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using monthly giving value proposition and defaulting to the recurring tab in the form for lapsed Heritage members will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because we capitalize on the importance of monthly giving and the impact their monthly gift will have on the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2024 - 03/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation sends traffic to their membership renewal page through the main website menu. The goal of this page is to have previous members renew their membership to the Heritage Foundation through a one-time or monthly gift.
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding value proposition language to the page about monthly giving would affect recurring donor conversion rates. In addition, we defaulted the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 179.1% lift in recurring donor conversion with a 96.6% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 24.2% decrease in one-time giving with only an 84.3% level of confidence.

The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Value Proposition Language Works**: Adding value proposition language about monthly giving effectively increased the conversion rate of recurring donors among lapsed Heritage Foundation members. This indicates that emphasizing the impact and importance of monthly giving can significantly influence donor behavior.

2. **Defaulting to Monthly Giving Tab Increases Conversion**: Defaulting the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option also contributed to the increase in recurring donor conversions. By making the monthly option the default choice, it likely nudged more donors towards selecting that option.

In future experimentation, the Heritage Foundation should continue to test different strategies for increasing donations among lapsed members. They should also consider applying the learnings from this experiment, such as using value proposition language and defaulting to the desired option, in other donation campaigns to see if similar results can be achieved. Additionally, ongoing testing and optimization of donation pages and strategies will help the organization continue to improve donor conversion rates and overall fundraising success.

When sending non-donor traffic to a donation page after signing a petition, we wondered if having a $200 option in the gift array was necessary or if would harm conversion by making non-donors have more options to consider, and feeling that if they could not make a large gift amount, then they should not give. We wondered if by reducing the friction in the process by taking out the highest option—which we surmised would be the lowest likely option for a non-donor to choose as their first gift—we would see an increase in conversion from this audience. experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array options for a non-donor audience led to a significant increase in conversions. By removing the highest option of $200 from the donation page, there was reduced friction in the decision-making process for potential donors, resulting in a 28.5% increase in transactions with 98% level of confidence for the non-donor traffic that qualified for entry in the test. This increase in conversions demonstrates that providing fewer options can help streamline the donation process and encourage more individuals to make a contribution.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of reducing the number of gift array options on conversion rates. This could involve further refining the donation page by eliminating additional high-end donation amounts or simplifying the overall layout to make it easier for potential donors to choose a gift amount. Additionally, it would be valuable to explore how this approach may impact donor retention and overall fundraising success in the long term. By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future testing and optimization strategies, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation can continue to improve its donation process and drive greater support for its cause.

By removing this ask from the copy and pre-selecting a default option in the gift array, the control version was able to provide a more frictionless giving experience and generate higher revenue.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of specific donation asks and pre-selected options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to test different donation amounts, language, and default options to optimize for higher revenue and average gift. Additionally, it would be prudent to carefully analyze the potential consequences of any changes in copy or design to ensure that they align with the objectives of the nonprofit and do not negatively impact donation performance.

experiments.title: How reducing decision friction in the gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the cognitive momentum will be improved with fewer choices and more social proof as guidance when choosing an amount from the array.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/29/2023
When converting a housefile offer to acquisition traffic, we reviewed the donation page and wondered if the gift array for the housefile was too complex for traffic that did not have as close of an affinity for the organization or have a previous history of giving. We wanted to test tailoring the gift array to the type of visitors we would be sending to the page and we wondered if a 6 option gift array with the majority of options over $100 would cause friction in the process for these prospective donors. We split traffic between the existing control gift array ranging from $25 to $25,000 and lowered the barrier for entry by making all gift options amounts under $100 as well as giving guidance/social proof pointing to the most popular selection to guide people seamlessly through the selection process and give more context for their choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic significantly increased donor conversion. By providing fewer choices and more social proof as guidance, the cognitive momentum of these visitors improved, leading to a higher likelihood of making a donation.
The treatment version, which included a simplified gift array with amounts under $100 and highlighted the most popular selection, resulted in a 26.1% increase in donations for all traffic, with a 96.2% level of confidence. Moreover, new visitors experienced a significant 261% increase in donations, and email visitors saw a remarkable 615% increase in donations, both with a 96% level of confidence.
To further leverage these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and optimizing the gift array for different traffic sources. By tailoring the gift options based on the visitors' affinity and previous giving history, we can further reduce decision friction and increase donor conversion.
Additionally, it would be valuable to explore other forms of social proof beyond highlighting the most popular selection. Testing different types of social proof, such as testimonials or impact statements, may provide further guidance to prospective donors and enhance their decision-making process.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the gift array to align with the preferences and needs of different traffic sources. By minimizing decision friction and providing guidance through social proof, we can maximize conversion rates and ultimately increase the number of donations for the Leadership Institute.

experiments.title: How adding a tab for recurring tribute giving affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tab for a recurring donation for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring gifts with no decrease in overall donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
Most of CaringBridge’s revenue came through their tribute donation page, which was driven by traffic to their journals. Because of the episodic nature of CaringBridge usage, donor retention was low — except for recurring donors. Since monthly recurring donors give smaller amounts regularly, they stick around a lot longer.
CaringBridge wanted to test including recurring giving on their donation page to see if it affected overall giving. They decided to allow it for 20% of traffic to mitigate any risk of decreasing overall giving. They did this via a “tabbed” form that gave the user a new “first choice” between a one-time and a recurring gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the treatment produced no statistically significant change in giving, while producing 8 recurring gifts from a very small slice of the traffic. It is interesting that the treatment drove a directional 3.7% lift in overall giving, which might suggest that the tab decision (which is a "smaller decision" than the gift array, increased overall engagement with the donation form.
This shows CaringBridge that they can safely include a recurring option without affecting overall giving, and produce valuable recurring donors from this funnel.

Because we had seen promising results on a similar test, we wanted to test reducing the number of options on a gift array for an organization’s French-speaking audience to see if they would also have an increase in conversion. The control donation page offered 5 donation amounts and an optional “Other” box to choose a gift of any amount. The treatment presented only a $10 ask and the “Other” field. We ran the test for 3 weeks splitting traffic evenly and measured results on donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for 3 weeks was the treatment produced a 116% lift in donations compared to the control with 96% level of confidence so the results are valid. We also saw a 136% lift in revenue with 98% level of confidence. However, we still had less than the minimum number of required transactions for these results to be statistically significant. experiments.title: How Removing Express Checkout Options from an Acquisition Donation Page impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the express giving option for acquisition donation pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the express checkout creates friction on the page . because  the express checkout creates friction on the page .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/20/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. In the past month, we noticed that the platform that they are using for donations recently rolled out an update that included “express giving options”. We wanted to test the impact this new feature has on their donor base and prospective donors who have not given to FOTF before.
We decided to run an A/B test showing half of the people who hit the landing page the old donation form version without the express giving option, and another half of the people who hit the donation pages the new form that contains the express giving option.
We hypothesized that the express giving option could cause friction in the donation process due to the lack of clarification on the form as to what these express options were and what benefit or value they provided the donor. While options like Google Pay (visible to any google device) and Apple Pay (visible to any Apple device) are more widely known to donors, Anedot pay is a newer feature specific to just Anedot donation pages and could add mental friction for simply being an unknown payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test showed that removing the express giving option resulted in a significant increase in donations, revenue, and conversion rate.
We do not believe this indicates that express giving options themselves are the problem but rather the options used and the way they are presented. Simply saying "express checkout" on the form leaves the user with more questions than answers as to how these options benefit them. Moreover, Anedot pay is likely too unknown to provide value to the user.
That is why we want to test a version of the form that presents just Apple Pay and Google Pay with a value driven headline. Perhaps, "Save time filling out this form. Give quickly and securely with just 1-click using one of our express giving options."
We will test this next.

experiments.title: How a monthly-first tabbed donation form affected monthly gift conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge’s main donation page received a steady but low volume of donations. They wanted to test ideas that would convert more of these gifts, which were mostly episodic gifts, into monthly recurring gifts. The monthly selection was below the donation amount selection, and they hypothesized that they needed to move the “one-time or monthly” selection above the gift amount. They also decided to make the donation form monthly by default, so that the user would select to move to a one-time gift. They launched a test of these two changes to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors led to a significant increase in donation conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 339.3% increase in donations compared to the control group. This was statistically significant with a confidence level of 99.9%.
These results suggest that making it easier for donors to choose a recurring donation option and pre-selecting it by default can lead to higher conversion rates.
In analyzing this experiment, the team wondered if the word "recurring" was more confusing than "monthly", and set up a test to better understand the difference in nomenclature.

experiments.title: How reducing friction in an instant donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor response because reducing the form length makes it feel like less work for the visitor and improves cognitive momentum.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 02/25/2023
The donation page software we were using rolled out a new update which changed the donation page template to offer express checkout options and allowed returning donors to “sign in” to complete their donation. We were curious if these changes to the donation page template were introducing unnecessary friction into the process and since these changes were automatically rolled out across all donation pages, we wanted to verify if the new template was helping or hurting donation page conversion. We developed a treatment that hid the express checkout and login options and kept all other elements of the donation page and form identical. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one week, we observed an overall 41% increase in donations with a 99.7% statistical level of confidence so the results are valid. Our hypothesis was correct that the newly implemented donation page template was hindering donation page conversion by increasing friction on the form. Additionally, we saw a 366% increase in revenue on the treatment version.
The key takeaway from this experiment is that reducing friction, such as login and express checkout options, and making the donation process simpler helped increase cognitive momentum in the process and improved donor conversion. To further validate the results, we should continue to test this same concept on other donation page placements outside of instant donation pages, including the main donation page and high urgency campaigns where visitor motivation may differ.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly giving donation tab for a recurring giving ask impacted recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the monthly giving donation form tab as the default tab  for potential donors  will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction. because  the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2023 - 02/18/2023
A public policy client asks users to make a monthly donation and join their monthly donor program after signing a petition. The ask details the monthly donor program, highlighting its purpose and the benefits of joining. But when the petition signers reach the donation form, the form defaults to a one-time gift ask. The form consists of two tabs, one for making a one-time gift and one for making a monthly gift to “Join the Come and Take It Society” – as the tab is labeled.
We wondered if the misalignment of making a recurring giving ask but defaulting the tabbed form to a one-time ask was causing friction, disrupting the user momentum, and possibly even causing confusion among potential donors. To test this line of thinking, we simply built a treatment that set the “Join the Come and Take It Society” tab as the default. Would this tweak result in an increase in monthly donors?
It’s also important to note that the monthly giving tab is supported by a monthly giving callout which reiterates some of the value proposition from the page copy and isolates several benefits of joining the Come and Take It Society.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a valid increase in recurring giving, producing four recurring gifts to the treatment and none to the control. This validates our hypothesis that potential donors benefit from congruency between the recurring donation ask and the recurring giving donation form tab. It makes sense for the recurring ask to be backed by a recurring giving opportunity through the donation form. It is also important for potential donors to see and read the recurring giving callout as it could very well add motivation for them to convert as a recurring donor. If the donation form defaults to one-time giving then there is a strong chance the user will not click to the recurring tab and they would therefore never have the chance to read the callout.
This test is worth testing on additional offers that include a recurring giving ask at the end of the funnel.
