experiments.title: How simplifying a gift array impacted lapsed donor reactivation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating a $10 ask on the gift array for lapsed donor survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/12/2022
We were running a survey to an audience of lapsed donors and followed the survey with an instant donation page. In previous testing, we had found that asking for a $10 gift in the body of the email copy increased donor conversion rate. We were interested in testing this same concept at the donation page level and hypothesized that reducing the number of choices on the gift array to only ask for a $10 gift would increase conversion rate from these lapsed donors. We removed all choices other than “$10” and “Other” from the gift array and split traffic equally between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed after running the experiment for 2 1/2 weeks was that there was a 79.8% increase in conversion rate with 99.5% level of confidence. We believe this is due to the reduced friction in the treatment experience where the donor did not have to pause and decide on an amount to give they could just decide "Do I want to give $10? Yes or no?" and continue their cognitive momentum from answering questions on the survey. We made the choice simple, reducing the perceived barrier in the decision-making process. While this treatment produced a better response in reactivating lapsed donors, in future testing we would like to test how this might impact new donor conversion as well. We also want to test increased gift amounts (such as $15 or $20) on the single gift array option to identify if it is the single choice or the lower amount that impacts results.

experiments.title: How adding language that conveys the value of joining a monthly newsletter near the form affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conveying the value of joining a monthly newsletter will increase email acquisition by giving visitors a tangible reason to sign up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/17/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates of website visitors on the homepage, we wanted to know if conveying the value of joining, and what visitors can expect to receive when signing up, would increase the rate at which people were willing to sign up.
Previously there was very little supporting copy near the signup form, with only the phrase “Subscribe to receive updates and event invitations.” presented in small font beneath the form.
For this test, we split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for just over two weeks before validating our results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 17 days, we were able to validate a 71.2%  lift in email acquisitions from visitors who saw the treatment page.
For the treatment, we wanted to convey enough value, social proof, and exclusivity to give visitors an enticing reason to submit their email addresses and agree to receive email updates from this public policy organization.
To add social proof: we leveraged the considerable size of the organization's housefile (146,000+) to express to the visitor that many other people with the same concerns, values, and interests have already signed upTo add exclusivity: we made it clear that they would be receiving emails from Dr. Ben Carson, a well-known and admired public figure, when they signed up. We also used the verbal cue, "exclusive, subscriber-only content" to reinforce this exclusivityTo add value: we clarified what subscribers receive when they sign up ... updates, educational content that teaches America's founding principles and delivers expert explanations, key insights, and critical analysis on the toughest challenges facing our nation today.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that simply asking someone to give you their email address with a generic sign-up prompt is not enough to earn their trust, pique their interest, and motivate them to join. Instead, organizations should consider what value subscribers receive when they join (what's in it for them), and communicate exactly WHY someone should subscribe and WHAT sets your email updates apart from other organizations.
Email marketing remains one of the most cost-effective, powerful tools an organization has to grow their housefile, build advocacy, and convert them into generous donors ... but as people become more and more selective about what messages (and from whom) they want landing in their inbox, communicating "what's in it for them" is no longer an option.
By setting yourself apart and conveying clearly the value people can expect to receive when they sign up, your organization has a huge opportunity to significantly improve your email acquisition rates today!

experiments.title: How “locking” the donation form on mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will locking the donation form on a person’s mobile device screen impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2021 - 10/30/2021
Focus on the Family continues to test into new methods to optimize donor conversion on their primary donation form. One of their main objectives is to increase mobile donor conversion. In the past, they had tested into using a two step form on mobile devices that saw a directional lift of 22%. While that test didn’t validate, it did give us insight into the giving experience and impact a tactic like that could have. To continue to test into this idea, but see if we could pull the lever on something that might increase conversion even more and validate the results, we tested into breaking the donation process into steps AND “locking” the donation form on the mobile screen once a person scrolled to the form. At that point, a user would either have to exit out of the donation form or make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The multi-step locked screen donation process increased donor conversion significantly by 36%! The locked screen captured the users attention and required them to take an action - either to leave or to donate. Because of this, more people decided to give because there were only two options for them to decide between when given the opportunity to just scroll the donation page, exit, or give. This increased the conversion rate on mobile devices to over 9%!

Here are other notable changes due to the experiment:
New visitors had a 44.9% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 25.0% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How one-click giving for dual ask gifts impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.

However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.

experiments.title: How the gift frequency section on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2021 - 02/12/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment originally ran during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign and showed a negative impact on donor conversion from both variations. We proposed testing this again outside of year-end, during neutral motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Similar to when we tested these variations at year-end, there was a negative impact on donor conversion from both treatments. In fact, the most significant decrease (16%) in donor conversion came from the button variation. The checked boxes also saw a 10% dip in donations as well. This experiment is a difficult one to fully understand because the two variations that were developed were intended to bring clarity to the giving process - something that donors had communicated that they needed. Instead, we added friction to the giving process. We hypothesize that the current control requires no work at all. In fact, We think that donors don't really notice it or understand it so they just continue to progress down the giving form to make their gift. The two new variations may be making the donor pause and think about the action they want to take when they get to that section. Continued testing needs to be done to: 1) gain greater clarity to the motivation of the donor and elements of friction on the page 2) explore our hypothesis about the gift frequency section altogether. To do this, our next test is going to be to completely remove the "Choose your gift frequency" section and present a recurring option in a different way.

experiments.title: The impact of clarifying why we need their email address on a pledge form
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to the form around the email field for visitors to our page will achieve a greater email conversion rate.

For our primary pledge acquisition offer we wanted to test whether adding additional copy around the email field would clarify for the visitor why we needed their email address and lead to greater conversions.
We decided to add, (get updates about the fight against abortion and infanticide) next to the email field.
experiments.keyLearnings: People were far less motivated to complete the form when we added that copy. We believe that putting that additional text next to the email field caused people to think less about the pledge they were signing and more about a subscription for "updates," decreasing the conversion rate. It shifted the value proposition from standing against infanticide to email updates. We will explore other ways of letting people know they will get emails from us without decreasing conversion.

experiments.title: How a layout of premiums impacts donation conversions

rows.name: Call out 1 giving level, with others below

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting premiums in an alternate layouts for desktop viewers will achieve reduce friction, decision fatigue and therefore increase donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run an A/B/C test.
Historically, Pathway to Victory will present premium giving levels in a grid format however in viewing other sites with multiple offerings we decided to test different layouts of the premiums to see if we could remove friction and help with decision fatigue in choosing a giving level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we had enough desktop traffic to run an A/B/C test.
For the "linear" version we saw the best conversion rates in donations with an increase of 33% with a level of confidence of 97.1%. For new visitors, we saw a 74.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the linear version is a layout that removes friction for the prospective donor by being a format in one column. Viewers could easily view the options, see differences in the level offerings, and make a well informed decision.
For further testing, we are going to explore a "most popular" call out, test into the language and description of offers to help further drive clarity and remove any decision fatigue the viewer may experience.

experiments.title: How decreasing the ask amount on a content offer instant donation page impacted conversions (physical resource)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the gift array  for new names acquired will achieve an increase in transactions because we have decreased the perceived sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that has physical resources to acquire new names through Facebook and website traffic.
When a resource is requested, the individual completes a form to receive the item and is then directed to an instant donation page.
The gift array used on these instant donation pages was $25, $50, $75, $95.
As these are brand new people to Pathway to Victory, we wanted to see if reducing the array would increase motivation to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, while we did see a decrease in average gift, $27.24 vs $16.15, we saw an increase of 130.9% in transactions. This increase in transactions contributed to an increase of overall revenue by 37.8% with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that by providing a lower ask in the gift array, we reduced anxiety for potential new donors. As new contacts are acquired through these resources, a $25 ask can be perceived as a "risk" whereas a $10 ask is more conservative and less of a risk and sacrifice to new prospective donors.
For these donation pages, PTV does not have a minimum donation amount required therefore they experience many gifts that are $1-5. However, with this experiment, we saw a DECREASE in these low-level gifts and saw more donors willing to make a $10 gift.
Our hypothesis is that by having the ask as $10 and "risk" being decreased, then more individuals were willing to make the full donation of $10 or more rather than a donation of $1-5.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.8% increase with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making the phone number optional affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the phone number field optional for visitors to the Global Online Fundraising Scorecard page will achieve more report downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2021 - 02/19/2021
In this experiment, we had launched The Global Online Fundraising Scorecard in partnership with Salesforce.org. Salesforce often requires a phone number in order to follow-up with new contacts, but we wondered if we could actually get more total report downloads by making the phone number field optional.
The control used a required phone number field. The treatment used an optional phone number field.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The optional phone number field increases conversions by 20.6% at a 98% level of confidence. This is further evidence that requiring information that is "non-essential" to the visitor introduces friction that can keep someone from saying "yes" to your offer.
However, the caveat is that we ended up with fewer phone numbers from the treatment page.
The control version (required phone number) captured phone numbers for 30% of page traffic. The treatment (optional phone number) acquired the phone number for 16.3% of traffic.
A next step from this experiment would be to evaluate the quality of the phone numbers and follow-up calls from the "Required" version versus the "Optional." Intuition would say that phone numbers given willingly would be more receptive to a phone call. But phone numbers given when it was required would be less receptive. However, more analysis will be needed to determine this.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the recurring gift option may have impacted single gift donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would presenting a reinforcement as to why the donor should consider a recurring gift impact donor conversion or revenue by single or recurring gift types?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, American’s For Prosperity made available to concerned Americans an opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress as they planned a government response to provide citizens aid, as well as focused stimulus dollars to “restart the economy” again after the pandemic.
The open letter had become a wonderful engagement piece for the housefile, so they decided to open it up to prospective supporters to give them the opportunity to add their name alongside tens of thousands of other signers, as well.
Because the open letter was so popular, they decided to put a donation appeal behind it and test acquiring new donors, and did so very effectively.
But as time went on, they thought: “Would people activate a recurring gift if asked?” — so we decided to test it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 97.5% level of confidence, we identified that the single gift donor conversion rate decreased by -16.1% with this treatment.
With fewer donors coming through the single gift option, it's worth noting that the average gift of those going through the treatment (and being exposed to the organization's core value proposition in the recurring gift reinforcement message), we did notice an average gift that was 14% higher than the gifts collected in the control experience.
But how did this do overall at converting recurring donors?
It did slightly better—achieving a +39% increase in donors choosing the recurring gift option, and subsequently +90% more monthly recurring dollars, however neither of those metrics were validated in this study.
So, what is causing this decrease in single gift donor conversion? We actually believe that it is because the 50/50 traffic split is sending less motivated traffic to the treatment experience, rather than the control. However, the offer is time-relevant, and time is running out. With the need to maximize results before the opportunity is gone, we decided to pull the treatment and eliminate the potential threat to results that it was indeed the cause of decreased results.
Further experimentation with this treatment is advised when it has an opportunity to run for a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How an additional, clarifying form field affects webinar registrations

rows.name: Do you work for a nonprofit organization? (Required)
Do you work for a nonprofit organization? (Not Required)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking registrants if they work for a nonprofit decrease webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 01/25/2019
In this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an additional field to understand whether or not someone worked for a nonprofit would make a significant impact on conversion. If we could add this field without hurting registrations, we’d be able to have cleaner data and do additional segmentation in our marketing to try to engage “for-profit” people on our email file differently.
So we set up a 3 variant experiment – the control had our normal form fields, variant 1 had an additional required field asking if someone worked for a nonprofit, and variant 2 had an un-required field asking the same question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result of this experiment was actually shocking. The best case scenario we hoped for was seeing no difference in conversion rate. Instead, we saw a 24% increase with the required field, and a 32% increase with the un-required field. Both increases reached 95% or higher statistical confidence. Variant 2 reached 99.9% confidence.
While I can't say I'm 100% confident as to why this increase occurred, my hypothesis is that it is very similar to this experiment with Jews for Jesus. Additional form fields often lead to an increase in friction - which results in lower conversion rates. But in this case, the additional question may have served as a qualifying question that gave registrants more confidence that the webinar is specifically geared for them. By sharing that they work for a nonprofit or for-profit, the implication is that the webinar will be more relevant and valuable.
The increase in perceived value outweighed the additional cost created by the form field, leading to greater conversion.

What the Results Suggest
Two behavioral patterns help explain the lift:
The middle effect: Donors tend to gravitate toward middle options. Raising the array raised that middle choice.
 	Perceived appropriateness: The uptick in conversion suggests higher amounts may actually feel more fitting for the cause — a lower ask, like $25, may signal that the need isn't serious.
Next Steps:
Given the consistent directional lift across conversion, RPV, and average gift, and acknowledging that limited sample sizes reduce confidence in the findings, this test warrants further validation with a larger sample — particularly for desktop and recurring giving, where data was too limited to draw robust conclusions.

experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: Does the placement of the payment module impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe moving the module to the end of the form for donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2025 - 04/21/2025
Due to a PCI update from our donation form vendor, the payment module now loads more slowly. We aimed to test where to place this module on the form to avoid adding friction. Our hypothesis was that placing the slow-loading module near the top would increase user friction and reduce conversions. To address this, we moved the module to the last step, giving it time to load.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of repositioning the PCI payment module within the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the module near the top of the form was causing friction due to its slow load time, leading users to encounter an unresponsive payment field before it fully rendered. By moving the module to the final step, we aimed to give it sufficient time to load before the user needed to interact with it.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate: +18% lift (80% confidence, meaning there is an 80% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
 	Overall Revenue Per Visitor: +8% lift (64% confidence, meaning there is a 64% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
Insights:
The improvement in conversion rate reflects a meaningfully smoother customer journey. Repositioning the payment module to the last step ensures it is fully loaded by the time users reach it, eliminating a key point of friction. Mobile results showed significant gains, while desktop RPV declined and warrants further investigation.
Recommendation & Next Steps
Based on these results, we have moved the module to the bottom of all top-trafficked donation forms and will adopt this placement as the default for new forms going forward.

experiments.title: Does pre-selecting higher default gift amounts within donation tiers increase overall revenue while maintaining average gift value?”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and relies on reader-supported giving to fund its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover sought to optimize its donation form experience to increase revenue per visitor while maintaining stable average gift values—particularly for prospect-heavy acquisition traffic.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how different gift arrays and preselection strategies affect conversion and gift value. For this test, we combined two variables: gift array size ($25/50/100 vs. $50/100/250) and preselecting the second gift amount on both arrays, resulting in four total variants. The test was run across multiple content offers to build sample size and understand how performance varied by offer type.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
directional but not statistically valid
Performance metrics
Total samples: 364
 	Total donations: 25
 	Overall average gift: $33.40
 	Control array ($50/100/250) was the lowest-performing variant on both transactions and revenue per visitor
 	Lower array variants ($25/50/100) showed higher conversion rates and higher revenue per visitor than control in some treatments
Audience and offer context
Baseline AOV for this offer is ~$40
 	~70% of traffic is from prospecting audiences
 	Preselection did not materially change performance for this offer or audience mix

experiments.title: How adding an option to donate through ACH impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ACH option for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the donation form’s payment section. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option, but would improve the user experience for those who would interact with it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the payment section on the donation form. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option but would create a better user experience for the users who would interact with it.

Key Results:

-       +10% non-statistically significant lift (81%) in overall conversion rate.

-       No change in overall RPV.

This test showed that by offering another familiar, often trusted option, we removed a potential barrier for donors who prefer or are accustomed to direct debit. Also, direct debit is often perceived as more secure and reliable, so the mere presence of an additional trusted option reduced friction for all users and made more donors comfortable completing the gift.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have implemented the direct debit option across all donation forms.

experiments.title: How preselecting the gift frequency and gift amount on a giving form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting and expanding the gift array for Today in the Word acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help reduce decision friction for donors by clearly presenting the preferred giving frequency and gift size within the acquisition funnel .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/24/2025
Moody Bible’s giving forms have historically been designed without pre-selected gift frequencies or amounts. While this has always been their standard setup, it differs from many other donation platforms where defaults are commonly used.
To test whether defaults could reduce decision friction and encourage giving, we introduced two pre-selections:
Gift frequency: defaulted to one-time
Gift amount: defaulted to the lowest option in the gift array ($25)
Our goal was to streamline the donor experience—minimizing effort and uncertainty—while also testing whether this change could increase overall conversion and potentially lift the average gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that pre-selecting gift frequency and amount—combined with expanding the gift array options—produced a significant lift in donor conversion metrics.
The treatment group outperformed the control with a 454.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic (94.4% LOC) and a 1,015% increase in Revenue (100% LOC).
 	Notably, while the sample size was small, the treatment showed no negative impact on transactions or revenue, indicating a clear positive response from Today in the Word’s audience.
 	The average gift amount also increased by 116.7%, confirming our hypothesis that the treatment would encourage larger donations.

experiments.title: How a tabbed form on the main donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tabbed form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the tabbed form provides more clarity on what type of gift the user is giving, therefore reducing potential friction while giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/13/2025
For Time of Grace’s main donation page, we wanted to add a tabbed gift array. We previously tested this, but we made some changes to this version. The first version said “Single Gift” for one-time gifts, and also had a dark green color for the selected tabs which was a bit confusing. So for this version, we changed the copy to say “One-Time Gift” and changed the selected tab color to be the gradient green they have on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The addition of a tabbed gift array on the main donation page resulted in a 46.3% increase in transactions for all traffic, showing potential clarity on the type of gift reducing potential friction in the donation process.
 	The treatment group also saw a significant 659% increase in One-Time Donation transactions, indicating that the tabbed form may have been particularly effective for this type of donation.
 	Despite the positive results, the statistical confidence level of 92.3% for All Traffic is slightly below the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the tabbed gift array.
 	Future experimentation should focus on refining the tabbed gift array design based on these results, such as adjusting colors, copy, or layout to further optimize the donation experience. Additionally, conducting additional tests with a larger sample size could help validate the initial findings and provide more conclusive results.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for ebook downloaders will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/13/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Guide to the Constitution eBook offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that changing the tab titles on a donation form to add clarity and donor autonomy led to a 32.6% increase in overall donor conversion rate at a 91.2% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%, the results were approaching validity. The treatment version, which used the language "Make a One-Time Gift" and "Make a Monthly Gift", was rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations of tab titles and messaging on donation forms to further evaluate their impact on donor conversion rates. It would be beneficial to explore additional iterations of language that provide clear guidance and empower donors to make informed decisions. Additionally, considering other elements of the donation form, such as design, adding icons that resonate with the donor's motivation, and call-to-action buttons, in conjunction with the tab titles, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how to optimize the donation process for increased conversions.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/01/2024
As a follow up to previous experiments, we wanted to see if using a tabbed donation form for visitors to an acquisition donation page would improve donor conversion rate and recurring donor acquisition. In previous experiments, we found no difference in one-time giving, but had seen 99% lift in recurring giving. However this was tested on the main donation page where motivation to give is very different than giving through an acquisition funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a tabbed donation form, resulted in a 29.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

2. While the statistical level of confidence (90.3%) is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, the results are approaching validity. This means that there is a strong indication that the treatment is effective, but further testing is needed to confirm its impact.

3. The previous experiment on the main donation page showed a 99% lift in recurring giving with a tabbed donation form. However, the current experiment on the acquisition donation page did not see a significant difference in recurring giving. This suggests that the motivation to give may differ between the two situations.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate its effectiveness. Consider running additional experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to improve statistical confidence.

Additionally, it may be valuable to explore the impact of different language and messaging on the donation form. Testing variations in messaging can help determine the most effective language that resonates with potential donors and increases conversion rates.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment suggest that a tabbed donation form has the potential to improve donor conversion rates on acquisition donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding a Security Callout to a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the security of an online gift with a callout line for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because eliminate a potential point of friction to giving — mainly the suspicion that sharing payment info or making an online gift isn’t secure.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 12/26/2023
CaringBridge’s Tribute Donation Page already has elements to reinforce security around the giving experience: a shaded box around the payment info and a lock icon. However, we hypothesized that an added line of copy in the payment section, “Your Information is Secure” would help tie together these elements, call out the security of an online gift, and increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a line of copy to reinforce the security of an online gift in the payment section of the Tribute Donation Page resulted in an 8.2% increase in donations. Although the results are not statistically valid, they are approaching validity with a 87.5% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a follow-up experiment with a larger sample size or longer duration to increase the level of confidence and validate the results.

Overall, the findings of this experiment suggest that calling out the security of the giving experience can help reduce any suspicions or concerns potential donors may have about sharing payment information. By addressing this potential point of friction, organizations like CaringBridge can potentially increase conversion rates and encourage more online donations.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page. because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How asking for less money on a parenting premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/05/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
We previously tested a lower gift array ($30 instead of $50) with FamilyLife’s Cherish Your Spouse series which led to a valid increase in donor conversion of 37.6% and an increase in the average gift amount. You can find that test here.
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether the impact of the lower gift array would be consistent across all verticals of Focus on the Family’s acquisition offers by running the same test on their Recognizing Your Son’s Need for Respect donation page (a parenting offer) and on Winning your Inner Battles (a faith offer). The results of the Winning Your Inner Battles test can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In our other experiments testing this lower gift array for the Cherish Your Spouse and Winning Your Inner Battles donation pages we saw validated increases in donor conversion, but during this experiment we weren't able to get greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic.
However, while this test did not validate any increase in donor conversion from the lower gift array, it did show in a directional lift of 68.8% with a 85% level of confidence. When looking at mobile traffic, the primary source for the offer, the LOC increased to 91%.  While this result does not meet the statistical level of confidence of 95% or higher, it does show a trend towards more users converting on the treatment version of the gift array. This result is in line with the results of the testing on Focus' other offers and reveals that, at least right now during high inflation, the lower gift array increases donor activation without harming revenue.

experiments.title: How validating each form field impacted overall completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding form field validation for petition signers will achieve an increase in petition signatures because the checkmarks will reduce friction by adding cognitive momentum and clarity in the user process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 12/16/2022
When reviewing petition signature pages, we wondered what how we could create a treatment that would provide additional cognitive momentum to reward visitors for beginning the process and encourage them to complete the remaining steps. We developed a green checkmark that would appear when someone had completed a required field to let them know they had successfully completed that step and could move on to the next field. If a user missed a step on either the control or the treatment, they could clearly see an error message that would prompt them to return to that form field and correctly complete it, but the treatment also interjected a “Looks good!” message to confirm they could keep moving forward. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding form field validation did not result in an increase in petition signatures for all traffic. However, the treatment did lead to a significant increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Moreover, the treatment showed mixed results across different visitor segments. For instance, there was a decrease in signups for mobile and email visitors, a significant increase in tablet visitors, and a considerable decrease in signups for female and male visitors.

Given these results, it is important to keep in mind that implementing form field validation may not improve petition signatures for all segments of visitors. Therefore, future experimentation should focus on identifying which visitor segments may benefit from this treatment and which segments may not. Additionally, the revenue increase observed in the treatment group should be further investigated to understand the underlying factors contributing to this effect. Lastly, it is worthwhile to continue experimenting with different design treatments that can reduce friction and encourage visitors to complete the signup process.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed donation form affect recurring conversion rates on a primary donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for donation page visitors will achieve higher recurring donation rates by making this giving option and its impact more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 02/10/2023
We wanted to know how a tabbed donation form (featuring single and monthly gifts) would affect recurring donations on the primary donation page for a public policy organization.
In this experiment, the control presented the option to make a recurring gift by featuring a checkbox below the gift array that asked the visitor if they would like to make their gift on a recurring basis. Beneath the checkbox, an impact statement communicated the impact of making a recurring gift.
The treatment instead presented the option to make a recurring gift in a tabbed form format, with “single gift” and “monthly gift” shown side-by-side above the gift array and the impact statement shown above the tabs.
For this experiment, ~50% of traffic was shown the control and ~50% of traffic was shown the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed that the tabbed donation form produced an 11.0% decrease in recurring gifts with a 25% level of confidence, a 25.4% decrease in overall donations with a 99.2% level of confidence, and a 32.1% decrease in traffic with a 94.4% level of confidence.
These decreases were most severe among returning visitors where total donations decreased by 29.5% with a 99.7% level of confidence and revenue decreased by 40.6% with a 97.6% level of confidence indicating.
For new visitors, we observed a large increase in revenue (177%) -- but because this increase was a result of just one additional donor, it is apparent that this is being skewed by a large gift and not indicative of overall performance.
These results disprove our hypothesis and indicate that in the context of visiting a primary donation page (high donor motivation), a tabbed donation form with the impact statement above the gift array may have distracted the donor or created friction by presenting multiple options before they made the decision to give.
It also suggests that it is more persuasive to ask someone to "upgrade" their donation after they have already made a micro-commitment by selecting their donation amount from the gift array.
Based on these results, we will look to test a monthly giving checkbox against the tabbed donation forms currently running on this organization's instant donation pages tied to acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How adding an email acquisition form directly to a relevant blog page impacted offer sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an offer sign up opportunity directly below a related and important blog article for page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2022 - 08/04/2022
Reasons to Believe built an offer in which users could welcome the organization’s newly appointed CEO by submitting an “encouraging note” to him. The offer was set up in its traditional way: After clicking on an ad or email link, users would go to a landing page where they would read about this opportunity to submit a personal note. They could then write the note and submit it after providing their first name, last name, and email address.
RTB also posted a blog article on the news of naming the successor to their longstanding president and CEO. We wanted to test how adding the same acquisition form below the blog content would impact email submissions. So instead of reading about the opportunity to submit a personal note, users would read about the news through the blog article and be given the opportunity to submit the personal note afterwards.
For the treatment, we added a bolded call-to-action after the blog article and then added the same acquisition form as we used in the control. We wanted to test which presentation of the offer would lead to more email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 60% lift in email conversions but only with a 72.5% level of confidence. The experiment did not validate. This offer could only run for a limited amount of time since it was news related. But it might be worth testing again in a similar capacity to see if there is in fact a trend in email conversion for a blog-level acquisition opportunity.
The experiment also revealed that returning visitors were twice as likely to convert than new visitors, which tells us that returning visitors are more familiar with RTB blogs and would perhaps get more out of reading a blog article about a new president taking over for the former president. If a similar offer is run again, it could be tested further for returning visitors.

experiments.title: Does the placement of a donation module on specific campaign pages impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module within content for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation module to the content section of a landing page versus adding it to the hero image. We hypothesized that adding it further down the page would allow new users to ingest our content, be motivated by it and then provide an easy option for donating at that point.
We tested this on various campaign pages—in this case campaign refers to specific areas where Save the Children operates (Ukraine, Gaza, South Sudan, etc)
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test compared the donation module's effectiveness in the hero section versus further down the page.
We hypothesized that placing the module lower on the page would give new users time to engage with the content, build motivation, and then encounter a natural opportunity to give.
Key Results:
Desktop conversion: ↑ 186% (91% confidence, not statistically significant)
 	Mobile conversion: ↓ 62% (97% confidence, statistically significant)
What Happened:
The results revealed a sharp split between desktop and mobile behavior.
On desktop, moving the donation module into the content showed a strong (though not statistically significant) lift in conversion. This suggests that desktop users may benefit from more time and context before being asked to give.
Overall, placing the donation module further down benefits desktop users but significantly reduces mobile conversions.

experiments.title: How removing the dollar ($) sign from a gift array impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe removing the dollar sign for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 07/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the dollar sign from the ask array levels on the donation form. We hypothesized that the dollar sign signaled the users brain to enter into transactional mode rather than staying connected to the emotion of the cause. Removing the dollar sign would allow the user to stay connected to the mission and would thus increase their willingness to give, potentially at a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We set out to test if the dollar sign adds friction for donors.
We thought seeing a “$” might feel transactional, deterring emotional giving. Removing it, we believed, could keep donors focused on the mission and increase giving.
What Happened:
The results told a very different story.
Instead of reducing friction, removing the dollar sign significantly hurt performance—especially on desktop. Both conversion rate and revenue per visitor dropped, in some cases dramatically.
This suggests the dollar sign isn’t a distraction. It’s actually doing something helpful: giving users clarity about what’s being asked of them.
Overall Performance:
Across all users, conversion dropped from 40.5% to 33%—a 17% decrease (52% confidence). Revenue per visitor declined even more sharply, falling 64% with 98% statistical confidence.
Desktop Performance:
The desktop saw the most dramatic impact. Conversion rate fell from 48.1% to 18%—a 62% decrease (95% confidence). Revenue per visitor dropped 75% (99% confidence).
It’s worth noting this segment had a small sample size (15 gifts), but the magnitude and consistency of the drop are hard to ignore.
Mobile Performance:
Mobile told a more mixed story. The conversion rate increased from 36.2% to 40.9% (a 13% lift), but with only 29% confidence, it is not statistically reliable.
At the same time, revenue per visitor dropped 58% (90% confidence), indicating that even when more users converted, they gave less.
One-Time Gifts:
For one-time donations, conversion declined from 32.4% to 24.2%—a 25% decrease (62% confidence). Revenue per visitor fell 69% (98% confidence), reinforcing the broader pattern: fewer gifts, and smaller ones.
Recurring Gifts:
Recurring performance showed a slight increase in conversion (8.1% to 9.1%), but with very low confidence (13%) and only 6 total gifts.
Revenue per visitor was essentially flat, decreasing just 0.97% (50% confidence). With such a small sample, these results aren’t directional.
Takeaway:
The main takeaway: keeping the dollar sign significantly improves donor clarity and response.
The dollar sign is not a barrier; it provides important clarity and guides users effectively through the donation process.
Without it, donors may feel uncertain, which can lower both participation and donation amounts.

experiments.title: Does Making the “Last Name” Field Optional Increase Email Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the “Last Name” field optional on content offer sign-up forms will increase email acquisition because reducing required effort lowers friction at the moment of decision and makes the form feel simpler and less intrusive to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2026
Luther Seminary uses its Illustrated Book of Acts and Enter the Bible Exodus content offers as ongoing acquisition tools to introduce new audiences to its ministry and grow its email list. These resources consistently attract individuals interested in biblical scholarship and theological education and serve as an important entry point into long term engagement.
As the team reviewed the landing page experience for these offers, they identified a potential source of friction within the sign up form. The form required three fields to be completed: first name, last name, and email address. While collecting a full name supports personalization and database management, required fields can introduce subtle resistance, especially for individuals who are early in their relationship with the organization.
This raised an important motivational question. When someone is downloading free theological content, are they willing to provide only the minimum required information, and does requiring a last name create enough additional effort or privacy concern to reduce completion rates?
Rather than removing the last name field entirely, Luther Seminary tested making the field optional while still requiring first name and email address. This approach preserved the opportunity to collect full names from those willing to provide them while lowering the minimum amount of required information needed to complete the form.
Two split URL tests were conducted, one for Illustrated Book of Acts and one for Enter the Bible Exodus. In each case, the page experience was identical except for the required fields on the form.
Control
First name required
Last name required
Email required
Treatment
First name required
Last name optional
Email required
Because both offers tested the same structural change with similar acquisition audiences, the results were aggregated to evaluate the overall impact of reducing required form fields across content campaigns.
The primary metric was email acquisition measured by form completion rate. Secondary metrics included overall transactions, one time gifts, recurring gifts, and revenue to ensure that any improvement in top of funnel performance did not negatively affect downstream donor value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test reached 77.2% statistical confidence, which does not meet the 95% level of confidence threshold for a valid learning. Therefore, we cannot conclude that making the “Last Name” field optional had a meaningful impact on email acquisition. The observed differences between control and treatment may be due to normal variation rather than a true behavioral effect.
