experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a tabbed form donation page affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly gift tab for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because describing what kind of impact the user could make with a monthly gift would encourage the user to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2025 - 10/31/2025
Time of Grace’s donation pages feature a tabbed form, allowing users to switch between one-time and monthly donations. We wanted to test adding a monthly callout to the monthly tab to see how that would affect recurring transactions.
The language described how the user could make a tangible impact by sharing Jesus’ love with people all year round through a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding a callout to the monthly gift tab on the donation page for visitors did not have the intended effect of increasing recurring donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 49.4% decrease in recurring donations across all traffic, with a 43.0% statistical significance level. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable.
There was an increase in the overall number of transactions.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully analyze the results and assess statistical significance before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the findings. It is also essential to consider factors such as the potential impact of the proposed changes on user behavior and donation patterns. Additionally, conducting A/B experiments for a longer duration and with a larger sample size may help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed form on acquisition donation pages impact donor conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed form for users on Time of Grace’s acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it helps remove unnecessary friction and choose how much they’d like to give after clarifying how often they’d like to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/16/2025
We’ve seen a tabbed donation form perform well for Time of Grace on their main donation page. We hypothesized that applying the same experience to their acquisition donation pages, receiving Facebook traffic for book premiums, would result in a similar increase. We believe that a tabbed form removes unnecessary friction, making it clear the type of gift a donor is making, which by extension can better clarify how much they may like to give and how often.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:The tabbed donation form on Time of Grace's acquisition donation pages resulted in a significant increase in transactions, with a 26.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, a larger takeaway is that revenue experienced a substantial 64.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This is a very promising result that shows the potential impact of the tabbed donation form on donor contributions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate the positive impact on donor conversion rates and revenue. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating learnings from this experiment, such as the design elements and user experience of the tabbed form, into future tests can help optimize the donation process and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: How removing the ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the processing fee option for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 05/31/2025
Many non-profit donation forms have a ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature where a donor can choose to increase their gift to cover any processing fees the non-profit might have to pay their payment providers. For Save the Children US, we wanted to test whether removing this function had an impact on conversion rates and revenue. We ran this one one of their evergreen donation forms, so the results were not influenced by any specific campaign driving traffic to that form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the processing fee option for potential donors resulted in a 5.2% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 41.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
Moreover, when reviewing overall performance based on revenue per visitor, the treatment also had a decrease (this removes large gifts), with a 37% decrease in revenue per visitor with a 99% level of confidence. This, however, makes sense, as the 'tip-up' function adds revenue to the overall gift.
Desktop:
Control: 30% conversion rate
Treatment: 24.6% conversion rate, which is a decrease of 18% with an 88% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 34% with a 95% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 14% conversion rate
Treatment: 17% conversion rate, which is a 20% increase with a 79% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor, however, deceased by 35% with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using an open amount field on a donation form impacts mid-level conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for potential mid level donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to see whether mid-level donors would respond more to a donation form with an open field instead of the standard gift array. The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for mid-level donors did not result in an increase in conversion rates and average gift as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in donations with a statistical confidence level of 34.5%, indicating that these results are not reliable or valid for decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequately large to increase the statistical confidence level. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of donation forms, including those with ask array amounts and open fields, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations from mid-level donors.

Overall, it is crucial to interpret results carefully and avoid making decisions based on inconclusive data. Further testing and refinement of donation forms may yield more reliable insights for optimizing fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: Does adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding other ways to give for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2024 - 09/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of adding additional ways to give, specifically Crypto, on donation forms. We added some language to the bottom of the form, under the ‘donate’ button, that gave donors links to be able to give through their preferred vehicle.
Our hypothesis was that adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form will make it easier for people to donate in their preferred method. This will decrease friction for the users looking for these options without impacting the overall conversion of the donation form for all other users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding additional ways to give at the bottom of a donation form did not result in the intended outcome of increasing conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical confidence level was not high enough to draw definitive conclusions.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of adding new elements to a donation form and to carefully assess whether these changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and conclusive results.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to conduct further research and testing to better understand the impact of offering different ways to donate on conversion rates. This could include gathering feedback from donors, conducting surveys, and running additional A/B tests with a larger sample size and more rigorous statistical analysis.

experiments.title: Does adding options to donate by crypto and DAF impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding crypto and DAF giving options for donation page visitors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2024 - 12/02/2024
This test explored the impact of adding DAF and Crypto buttons to the donation form. The goal was to increase the use of these payment methods without detracting from regular form conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding crypto and DAF giving options to the donation page did not have a significant impact on conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a slight decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for making future decisions. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical significance threshold is met before drawing conclusions. It may also be worth exploring other variables or factors that could potentially impact donation conversion rates and testing them in a controlled manner to determine their effectiveness.

experiments.title: How using No-primed questions in a survey impacted engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “No-primed” questions for a survey offer will achieve an increase in survey completions (email submissions) because respondents will feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers, which may lead to a subconscious desire to resolve with a “Yes” when asked to contribute later.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2024 - 11/27/2024
During high-urgency campaigns such as calendar year-end, we often use a survey as a tool to help “prime” Luther Seminary’s audience with knowledge about the problems that Luther Seminary is working to solve through their fundraising campaign. Typically, these survey questions are “yes-primed”, which means that they’re designed so that a Luther Seminary supporter would respond “Yes” to each question.
For Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test this “yes-primed” framing against a survey that was “no-primed”. Our goal was to understand how this “Yes” or “No” priming impacts engagement and downstream donor behavior.
Both survey funnels were identical, with the only change being the phrasing of the 5 survey questions. The Control survey used “Yes”-primed questions, which are designed to elicit affirmative responses from respondents. The Treatment survey, on the other hand, used “No”-primed questions, encouraging respondents to think critically about the topics and more likely to answer negatively.
This experiment aimed to explore the psychological principle of reactance theory, which suggests that when people feel their freedom of choice is being restricted or guided (such as through overly positive framing), they may react by resisting or disengaging. Conversely, by using “No”-primed questions, we hypothesized that respondents would feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers. The idea is that when people are encouraged to think critically and reject easy affirmations, they may feel more invested in their responses, fostering a stronger connection with the survey.
Additionally, answering “No” to these questions may create a cognitive dissonance effect, where respondents feel the need to resolve the negative stance with a positive action, such as donating after completing the survey. This subconscious desire for resolution may lead to higher engagement during the survey and increased donor conversions later in the funnel, especially when presented with an opportunity to act on their beliefs.
We split traffic to the survey pages evenly, and measured the # of survey submissions (emails acquired), as well as downstream donations on the post-survey donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test can be broken down into 3 key learnings:

 

#1 - Increase in Engagement with “No”-Primed Questions

The Treatment survey, which used "No"-primed questions, resulted in a 7.8% increase in form submissions compared to the Control survey. While this increase did not meet the 95% statistical level of confidence (79.1% LOC), it still suggests a promising trend toward higher engagement. This aligns with our hypothesis, which was grounded in reactance theory. By encouraging respondents to critically evaluate the questions and answer negatively, we likely fostered a greater sense of autonomy. Respondents who felt they were not being led to affirmations may have felt more invested in their responses, leading to increased engagement.

 

#2 - Potential Impact of “No” Priming on Conversion Behavior

Despite the increase in form submissions, the Conversion Rate for the Treatment group (2.41%) was slightly lower than the Control group (3.36%). This discrepancy suggests that while “No”-primed questions led to more people completing the survey, there might be a short-term resistance to following through with donations immediately after completing the survey. However, the cognitive dissonance effect mentioned in our hypothesis may still be at play—respondents who answered "No" to the questions may feel a subconscious need to resolve the negativity, and this could influence them to donate in the future as they reconcile their answers with the desire to support Luther Seminary.

 

#3 - Higher Average Gift in the Treatment Group

The Treatment group had a higher average gift of $32, compared to the Control group’s $23. This finding suggests that while the number of donors was slightly lower in the Treatment group, those who did choose to give were more likely to contribute larger amounts. This is potentially indicative of commitment consistency. Respondents who were engaged through the "No"-primed survey, and who felt more autonomous in their responses, may have been more motivated to make a larger, more deliberate contribution to Luther Seminary's cause. By thinking critically about their answers, they may have been more intentional in their giving.

 

The results of this experiment point toward the potential effectiveness of “No”-primed surveys for increasing engagement and stimulating larger gifts, even if immediate conversions didn’t significantly increase. Respondents who answered negatively may have felt more empowered and invested in their decisions, which could lead to higher-value gifts in the long term. Future tests could explore the extended impact of these psychological principles, with a focus on donor behavior over time and refining the optimal balance between engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: Does changing donation form copy to be more donor-focused affect conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor-focused language for users who downloaded the Fall Digest will improve conversion rates because emphasizing a sense of belonging and camaraderie taps into donors’ intrinsic motivations to contribute to a shared cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2024 - 01/11/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The experiment evaluated the impact of copy variations on the conversion rate of the Fall Digest instant donation page. The test focused on donor-focused language inspired by top-performing offers, with the goal of enhancing a sense of belonging and camaraderie to drive higher conversions. This experiment targeted users who downloaded the digital version of the Fall Digest and were directed to a donation page.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page where people can make a gift in order to become a member of Heritage. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from on-site buttons and popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the gift amount displayed saw a 0.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control version, but at a 3.92% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing different variations of the submit button language and design to see what impact they have on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run experiments for a longer duration and with more traffic to ensure reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a quiz affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a quiz will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 10/15/2024
The Heritage Foundation has an offer where people can take a quiz to test their knowledge on Marxism and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight the Marxist movement in America today. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that decreasing the gift array amounts from $50, $100, and $250 in the control group to $25, $50, and $100 in the treatment group did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, but at a 3.6% level of confidence, this result is invalid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before drawing any conclusions or making changes based on the results. This can be helped by increasing traffic to the test page. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the messaging, imagery, or targeting strategies used in the donation process. Conducting further experiments with different variables and analyzing the results with a higher level of confidence will help in making informed decisions to optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal thermometer to a year-end donation form impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For their year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether using a donor goal thermometer on the year-end donation form will impact donor conversions. In order to hit the $320,000 goal, Avila needed 500 donors to step up and make a donation. In this experiment, the control had no thermometer; the treatment automatically updated based on gifts to that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors resulted in a 19.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. While the treatment group did outperform the control group, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.0%, falling below the typical threshold of 95% for valid results.

Moving forward, it is important to note that these results should not be used for future decision-making as they do not meet the standard threshold for statistical significance. However, the experiment does provide some insights into the potential impact of using a donor thermometer on donor conversions.

For future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the use of a donor thermometer but with adjustments to the implementation or design in order to potentially achieve statistically significant results. Additionally, considering a longer duration for the experiment and a larger sample size may also help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout on the tributes donation array impacts conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic impact array for users on the tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it encourages interaction with the array and clearly outlines the impact of a donor’s gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that a dynamic impact array that automatically updated impact language based on the user’s gift selection would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamic impact array tested on the tribute donation page led to a 3.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.7%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is large enough to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more diverse data and account for any external factors that may impact the outcome.
While the initial results of the dynamic impact array seem promising, it is important to validate these findings with a more rigorous experiment before implementing any changes based on this data. Further testing with a larger sample size and a longer duration will provide more reliable insights into the impact of the dynamic impact array on donor conversion rate and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for high urgency campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running their yearly high-urgency President’s Challenge campaign. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was ultimately inconclusive. We learned that adding the donor's selected gift amount to the donation form's submit button may have a positive impact on donor conversion rate for high urgency campaigns. The treatment version, which included the gift amount in the submit button, saw a 1.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 12.5%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This means that we cannot confidently say that the increase in transactions was solely due to the change in the submit button language.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of including the gift amount in the submit button across multiple campaigns and audience segments to see if the results are consistent.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing is needed to confirm the impact of this change on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for eBook campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate. because  the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2024 - 11/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running ads for their Election Fraud eBook offer, where visitors fill out a form to receive a copy of the eBook and then are redirected to a donation page. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for the eBook campaign did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 7.4%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
Therefore, we should not make any conclusive decisions based on the results of this experiment. It is important to recognize that sometimes variations in performance may occur due to random chance rather than a true impact of the changes made. In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used and that statistical significance is reached before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other variations or factors that could potentially impact donor conversion rates to gain a better understanding of what drives donor behavior.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have signed up to receive an eBook will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 09/12/2024
An organization has an offer where people sign up to receive an eBook and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the amounts on the gift array may have a positive impact on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had lower gift amount options of $10, $25, and $50, saw a 9.4% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 61.6% is below the threshold of 95%, meaning that these results are invalid and may not be reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, it could be useful to test different variations of gift amounts to see which combination yields the best results in terms of donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment suggest that lowering the gift array amounts can potentially increase donor conversion rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How more applicable tab titles on a donation form + an action oriented statement impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donation form tab title from recurring to monthly and implementing action-orienting statements for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the word “monthly” will better resonate with their audience and the action-oriented statement prompts the visitor to take said action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2024 - 11/21/2024
The Navigators run a monthly Scripture Memory Text challenge. After participants sign up to join the challenge, they are asked for a gift.
We noticed that The Navigators tend to use the word “monthly” rather than “recurring” however their donation form says “recurring.”
We thought that this may be causing unnecessary friction on the donation page, and we saw it as an opportunity to also test into action statements for the donation form tab titles.
Control: One-time Gift & Recurring Gift
V1: One-time Gift & Monthly Gift
V2: Make a One-time Gift & Make a Monthly Gift
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the donation form tab title from "recurring" to "monthly" did not have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 21.6% statistical level of confidence.

These results suggest that the original tab title of "recurring" may have resonated better with the audience, despite the preference for using the word "monthly" in other communication from The Navigators. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that these results cannot be considered statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the language used in communication and donation forms, taking into account the preferences and expectations of the target audience. Additionally, it is advisable to conduct further A/B testing with a larger sample size and potentially try different variations of the tab titles to determine the most effective messaging for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field to a donation form impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a phone number field to the donation page for donation page vistors will achieve a similar amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 08/09/2024
We wanted to start sending a thank you voicemail to donors after they donated, but to be able to do this we needed to start collecting phone numbers on the donation page. Before rolling out this change to the donation page, we wanted to make sure it wouldn’t impact the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a phone number field to the donation page resulted in a 7.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to adhere to a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an experiment. This will help to avoid making decisions that may not actually have a meaningful impact on the desired outcome.

experiments.title: Will Adding ACH as the Preferred Method of Payment Decrease Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by showing ACH as the preferred of payment for donors coming the monthly giving page will achieve longer donor retention in the long run because banking information expires with less frequency over credits cards. An added benefit is the reduction in credit card processing fees as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
An organization worked on identifying ways to retain their monthly partners long-term. Because bank account information vs. debit/credit card information tends to change or expire less often than credit cards, will we retain our donors longer? Simultaneously, we also need to see if showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page hurts our current donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page resulted in a 6.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This shows us an important finding. Including ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly donation page DOES NOT hurt donor conversion and can be considered for monthly giving pages as the preferred method of payment as it will help retain monthly partners longer.

experiments.title: How condensing a form and using more positive copy for a content offer impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the landing page form and using more positive copy for prayer calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the form will be perceived as less questions to fill out and the positive tone will resonate more with the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s main Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a 3-way test. Treatment 1 condensed the landing page form so that it would be perceived as shorter for the user, and treatment 2 also condensed the form as well as used a more positive tone in the copy by changing the first line of the body copy.
Our hypothesis was that both of these changes would increase the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that both condensing the landing page form and using a more positive tone in the copy had little to no impact on the email acquisition rate for the Prayer Calendar landing page.

While these were not the results we were anticipating, it is interesting that the more positive copy did not have more of an impact on the landing page visitor.

Although we did not get any valid metrics from this test, it does tell us that the long form on the landing page does not negatively impact the visitor's motivation to complete the landing page.

We plan to continually run experiments on this funnel and test the motivations of IMB's audience.

experiments.title: How switching the order in which information is presented affects conversion rate

rows.name: Switch order of the Main Donation Page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by switching the order in which information is presented for our donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2021 - 10/24/2021
We tested this on FamilyLife’s main donation page. We noticed that when filling out your information, the last step to complete the donation process is adding your card information. We suspected that this could potentially cause friction due to all the internal decisions we are already making before hitting “make my gift”. Could be not having your card ready to fill out that information or perhaps your willingness to make that “commitment” decreases as you fill in other information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 57.3% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the impact of a gift impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including this statement of value proposition for visitors will achieve an increase in the number of transactions because the visitor will be reminded about the impact that their gift could make within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2023 - 11/27/2023
In an effort to increase the number of donations for ACI, we wanted to test adding additional value proposition within the donation form. We tested this on a multitude of pages with varying content and offers ranging from the homepage donation page, pro-life pledge donation page, education survey donation page, and a few others. With this being said, each different topic had a different value proposition statement that was relevant. Here are the statements that we used:
Pro-life: Your donation today will help fight for the rights of unborn children across the nation.
Education: Your ongoing support will help educate thousands of children on the true principles of America’s founding throughout the year.
General: Your recurring monthly gift will defend our nation’s guiding principles of life, liberty, community, and faith 365 days a year.
Little Patriots: The entirety of your gift will directly support our efforts to provide free curricula and cartoons that teach true American history through our Little Patriots program.
This statement was shown under the tabbed options of “One-time” or “Monthly,” and it was shown regardless of which tab the visitor had selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition statement within the donation form had a slightly positive impact on the conversion rate for transactions at 4.9%.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 67.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the impact of value proposition statements on donation conversions, using larger sample sizes and longer experiment durations. Additionally, it would be valuable to determine whether the recurring specific statements were more or less beneficial in the future.

By conducting more rigorous experiments and increasing the statistical level of confidence, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights on how to optimize the donation form and increase the number of donations for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How an embed form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an embedded donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an embedded form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the embedded form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an embedded donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

In terms of revenue, the control produced $3,555 in revenue and the treatment produced $1930. Therefore the control had an average gift of $148 and the treatment produced an average gift of $77. This produced a 44.8% drop in revenue at a 65.3% level of confidence, also considered invalid.

Due to the lack of validity in results, these results indicate that the embedded made little to no difference in the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How a embedded form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an IDonate embed donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.

Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an IDonate embed form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the IDonate embed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an IDonate embed donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

These results indicate that the IDonate embed made little to no difference on the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience. It is important to note that the revenue is not accurate in the above graph due to the fact that revenue from the IDonate Embed does not flow into Winston.
