experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form tab affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s monthly gift tab and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2024 - 06/11/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
Wondering whether this concept would appeal to donors on another direct appeal donation page, they also tested this flag image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button, which led to a decrease in donations at a near-valid level of confidence.
As a follow-up experiment on the direct appeal donation page, they wanted to test whether the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, namely recurring donations.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's monthly gift tab and submit button did not lead to a valid increase in donations, specifically recurring donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 40.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of 78.8% is below the valid threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable.

The treatment also resulted in just a 0.17% increase in recurring donations at a 68.3% level of confidence. With such a small sample size, these results cannot be relied upon to inform future action.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a statistically valid result. Additionally, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as flag icons on donor behavior. Other ideas for follow-up testing include testing a different icon than an American flag or testing this concept on a donation page following a content offer.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount to a main donation page form submit button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for main donation page donors will achieve an increase in donation rate because increased clarity and donor autonomy will persuade donors to follow through with making their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase donation rate on their main donation page, The Heritage Foundation wondered whether adding the donor’s gift amount in the donation form submit button would increase clarity and donor autonomy and lead to this increase.
The control form submit button read “MAKE MY GIFT” while the treatment button read “MAKE MY GIFT OF $(amount)”.
Each variant in this A/B experiment received 50% of traffic which ran for 24 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button on the main donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.0%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Future experimentation should include running this test on a donation page that receives more traffic, such as a content offer.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the submit button text or other elements on the donation page to identify what truly resonates with donors and leads to an increase in donations.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for survey takers will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 05/14/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Voter Fraud Survey offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that clarifying the language in the tab titles of a donation form can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates, both for one-time donations and recurring donations. The treatment version, which used clearer language ("Make a One-Time Gift", "Make a Monthly Gift"), saw a slight 0.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Interestingly, the same treatment did lead to an increase in email conversion rate on a donation page following an ebook offer. This suggests that the treatment tab titles may resonate more strongly with people downloading an ebook as opposed to people who have taken a survey.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of language clarity in survey donation forms on conversion rates, ensuring that statistical significance is achieved to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the design, layout, and call-to-action language in donation forms could also be valuable for optimizing conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a multi-step form impact donation conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a single step form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversions because previous research suggests that well-optimized single step form out performs a multi-step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2024
In this experiment, George W Bush Presidential Center had just migrated to the iDonate platform. Their previous donation page used a long-form, single page donation form. When migrating to the iDonate platform, they opted to roll-out a more streamlined multi-step donation form—but they wanted to test the impact of the multi-step widget versus a single step form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that in this specific case, the single step form for main donation page visitors did not achieve an increase in conversions as hypothesized. But with a level of confidence of 52%, the likelihood is that there is no significant difference in performance between the control and the treatment.

Bush Center can move forward knowing the moving to a multi-step for is unlikely to harm results. In fact, there's a chance that it may be the more effective treatment.

With low conversion volume on the donation form, future experimentation should be focused on driving more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the titles of a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for previous members will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a membership renewal page where previous members of the organization can renew their membership either through a monthly gift or a one-time gift.
They wanted to test adding clarity to the titles of the donation form to see if it would increase the recurring conversion rate and one-time donor conversion rate.
The control monthly tab is labeled “Join Leaders Club” and the one-time tab is labeled “Single Gift.” For the treatment, we clarified on the monthly tab that they could “Make a monthly donation” and for the one-time tab they could “Make a one-time donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding clarity to the titles of the donation form tabs did not have an effect on donor conversion rates for one-time or monthly giving. We saw that clarifying the monthly tab to "Make a monthly donation" led to an 11.8% increase in recurring transactions but with only a 19% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 5.4% lift in one-time giving, but with only a 13% level of confidence.

This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the donation form titles to further optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results. By continuously testing and refining different elements of the donation process, the organization can work towards maximizing donor conversion rates and ultimately increasing support for its cause.

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: Removing Express Checkout on the Sustainer Campaign Giving Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing express checkout for HUC donation pages will achieve a higher percentage of conversion because there will be less decision friction once a donor hits the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 03/12/2024
On previous tests for FOTF, we’ve seen varying degrees of success when testing the removal or decrease in size of the Express Checkout method of payment on FOTF-giving pages. Would removing the Express Checkout on the Sustainer campaign giving pages also yield the same results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Removing the Express Checkout for the Sustainer campaign giving pages resulted in a significant decrease in Transaction - Recurring by 60.1%.
2. There was a 61.2% statistical level of confidence in the result for Transaction - Recurring, indicating that the control group performed better.
3. The Transactions - One-Time experienced an 861% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and Transactions experienced an 840% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Therefore, it is not recommended to make decisions solely based on these results. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to reconsider the hypothesis and perhaps conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to draw more conclusive findings.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated tabbed form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because there is additional clarity in the process by adding frequency choice before the gift array choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 03/18/2024
When sending pledge signers to an instant donation page, we wondered if emphasizing the monthly gift option by creating a tabbed form experience would increase recurring donor conversion. We split traffic between the two versions and ran for the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the animated tabbed form for the instant donation page resulted in a 50.3% increase in recurring donation for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As a result, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to determine the most effective ways to increase recurring donor conversion. It may be beneficial to explore other design and messaging variations in future experiments to see if they have a significant impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a “Most Popular Gift” call out on the gift array impacts one-time donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a “Most Popular Gift” call out for donation page gift arrays will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is.. because  it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2024 - 02/23/2024
Luther Seminary has seen a steady amount of traffic on their donation page. Conversions have been consistent, but the average gift has been lower. We wanted to see if we could get more conversions and also increase the number of gifts because we increase clarity of what the most popular option is for most page visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Most Popular Gift" call out to the donation page gift arrays led to a 22.0% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 77.3% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and account for any potential fluctuations.
Overall, the concept of highlighting a "Most Popular Gift" option shows promise in potentially increasing donor conversions and gift amounts. It could be worth conducting further experiments with adjustments to ensure reliable and conclusive results that can guide decision-making for optimizing donation pages in the future.

experiments.title: How providing form validation impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding validation that a field is completed correctly for web donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is visual confirmation of correct information.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2024 - 02/12/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
We decided to test and see if by adding validation on each form field would generate a higher conversion rate due to the confidence of completing the form correctly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding validation to fields for web donors may potentially increase the conversion rate. While the treatment group did see a 0.1% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is adequate before drawing any conclusions. This can be done by increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time. Additionally, it would be beneficial to evaluate other factors that may impact conversion rates, such as the user experience, website design, and donation process flow. By considering these factors and conducting experiments with sufficient statistical power, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights for improving conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on a primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 12/12/2023
We previously tested how a tabbed donation form affected recurring donations on a public policy organization’s main donation page. In this experiment, the tabbed donation form produced an 11% decrease in recurring gifts (with a 25% level of confidence) and a 25.4% decrease in overall donations (94.4% level of confidence), which disproved our hypothesis that a tabbed donation form would achieve higher recurring donation rates than a monthly giving checkbox by making the monthly giving option and its impact more visible.
In an effort to learn whether a tabbed donation form on this organization’s main donation page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate, we retested this idea. The control in this experiment presents a monthly giving option as a checkbox below the gift array, while the treatment has two tabs for either a Single Gift or Monthly Gift with a value proposition callout box above the monthly gift tab. 50% of traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we observed that the tabbed donation form on the main donation page led to an insignificant 2.2% increase in overall donor conversion rates, but its 11.3% level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Therefore, we cannot confidently conclude that the tabbed donation form is more effective in increasing conversions, but we also cannot conclude that it is less effective.
Additionally, the treatment saw a 99.5% increase in recurring donations, but at an invalid 58.4% level of confidence.
These results, along with data of tabbed donation forms leading to an increase in donations in the organization's acquisition offers funnels, may indicate that people visiting their main donation page may have high donor motivation and be less influenced or even more distracted by the tabbed design of the form. The results also may have been impacted by an additional variable—the control not having the value proposition callout box found in the treatment's experience.

experiments.title: How a larger impact number affects donor conversion on the main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will increase their perception of Heritage’s impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2023 - 11/06/2023
The Heritage Foundation had long used their 500k member base as a leg of their value proposition to show their broad support. However, their marketing team’s outreach initiatives meant that they actually reached a lot more people each year than they had members.
Donors responded very favorably to this “larger” metric, but they wanted to see if that proved out in increased fundraising results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors, the conversion rate can be increased. This is supported by the fact that the treatment version, which presented the larger number of 10 million members, advocates, and concerned Americans reached per day, resulted in a 7.7% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 34.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Based on this, it is recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact of showing a larger number on the conversion rate. The future experimentation should involve a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the language used to present the impact number to see if different phrasing or positioning can further improve the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How lowering a gift array on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower gift array aligns better with the perceived value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
IMB is running an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads to sign a note of encouragement for missionaries who are traveling and out in the field. They wanted to increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page of the acquisition offer so we decided to lower the gift array because this is a low value offer and they aren’t receiving in return like an ebook funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the lower gift array saw a significant decrease in donations of 49.3% compared to the control version.
Based on these results, it is evident that the perceived value of the offer did not align with the lower gift array. This suggests that donors may be more willing to donate higher amounts when presented with a wider range of options. Or it suggests that users see this content offer as high value and makes them want to give more to support more missionaries.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the perceived value of the offer and ensure that the gift array aligns with this value. It may be beneficial to test different gift array options to find the optimal balance between a wide range of options and the perceived value.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.
Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button color on a homepage affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the donate button color in the menu for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because people will have less friction finding where to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 07/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to test changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu for this prison ministry client. The control color of the donate button is part of the color palette that this brand has. Our idea was to find a color that is different and would make the word “Donate” a lot more obvious to someone looking at the site. If the donate button stands out more, we believed that it would increase the conversion rate since the donor can see the button quicker and have less friction finding the donation button.
For this experiment, the only difference is that the control page has the original color donate button and the treatment has a new color. We split this test 50/50 as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu resulted in a 29.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.9%, which is not validated since it is below the 95% standard level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test a more radical design difference for the donate button to identify the most effective option. This could include shape, size, button animations, and font. By adding a more drastic design difference to the donate button, this could draw more donors which could increase the conversion rate. A/B testing should continue to be conducted, comparing different design elements or variations to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How increased perceived security impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding additional copy for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion because there is perceived higher security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 06/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
To increase donations and revenue, we decided to test increasing the perceived security of the donation form for PTV’s 18 Minutes with Jesus book
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we only saw a 4% increase with a 53% confidence level.
Due to the inconclusive results, we are going to test other methods of security such as an animated lock.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving process from 2 pages to 1 page impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experience to one step  for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because reducing steps to a process can reduce friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2023
In a previous experiment, the Navigators successfully increased donor conversion rate by reducing the number of steps in the giving process from 3 to 2. Our hypothesis was that we would reduce even more friction in the giving process by consolidating the experience into a single page. Unfortunately, the organization’s current giving platform was not able to accommodate a single step giving experience. Therefore, we used a completely different platform for this experiment’s treatment. We kept the control as-is in its current platform and the treatment on a new platform as a single-page experience.
In a previous iteration of this experiment we saw a valid 55% decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that the user may have experienced a break in branding congruity. So, we rebuilt this experiment on another offer and made the donation page look more like the original donation page in terms of colors and aesthetics.
We only ran this experiment in one ebook offer funnel and most of the traffic was driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73%, indicating that the results are not valid.
Though slightly better in results from our previous experiment, we're still seeing a decrease in donor conversion rate which was not expected.
Additionally, we thought that the control might be more successful in converting returning donors because there is a "log-in" feature in the original platform that may be a convenient way for pre-existing donors to give. However, when looking at new vs. returning donors in each segment, there is virtually no impact in conversion rate among each segment.
Ultimately, we believe that there are too many new variables introduced in a completely new platform that could've impacted giving. It may not be that reducing the experience down to 1 step is reducing conversion rate. Other features like not being able to personalize the first name, or including Anedot express giving/Google pay, are features we did not initially account for.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by shortening survey length impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of a survey will increase email acquisition rates by reducing friction and capitalizing on the cognitive momentum created by the ad that sends traffic to the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2023 - 03/17/2023
When reviewing results of a survey offer funnel, we observed a solid click-through rate, a low cost per click, and a fair instant conversion rate indicating that this is an offer and an issue people care to engage with. However, we identified lower-than-average email acquisition rates as an opportunity to improve overall results. After reviewing this campaign, we believe that the length of the survey was introducing significant friction and causing visitors to abandon. In all, the survey contains 20 questions. In a previous experiment, we were able to produce a sizable lift in survey completions by paginating the survey, rather than presenting all 20 questions on one page. However, over time, email acquisition was still struggling for this offer and we believe that its length was causing the cognitive momentum in landing page visitors to fizzle out. To test this theory, we built a treatment that truncated the survey to ~5 questions all presented on one page to reduce friction/cost of completing survey in effort to improve email acquisition. We split traffic 50/50 between the two versions and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month we saw a 0.86% increase in conversion rate with 12.4% level of confidence. However, we learned that not all friction is bad friction because we saw a significant decrease in Donations and Revenue, with a 100% and 98% level of confidence, respectively. This suggests that reducing the length of the survey may have a negative impact on the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. When reviewing downstream effects on donations, the treatment produced a 90% decrease in gifts with 99% level of confidence. We believe this happened because the more time that was invested in the survey, the more willing the visitor was to convert on the instant donation page. This means the control version with a lengthier survey was actually producing more qualified traffic to the donation page. Because the results were overall flat at the landing page level, we will need to do additional testing to improve email acquisition rate while still maintaining the quality of the visitor at the donation page level. In future testing, we could add additional elements to the process to let visitors know how many steps there are to complete the survey and display their progress along the way so they have more clarity around how long it will take them to finish the survey and submit their responses. It is important to note that we saw a significant increase in email acquisition rates for Housefile Email visitors, suggesting that the treatment may have a positive impact on visitors who are already familiar with our organization. Future experimentation should focus on finding a balance between reducing friction and maintaining the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. We can also consider targeting Housefile Email visitors with a truncated survey to capitalize on their familiarity with our organization and potentially increase email acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How conversational headers on a donation form impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating conversational headers for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because people will treat purely digital experiences like they are alive in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/09/2022
When reviewing a donation form post-petition, we realized the conversational nature of the donation page language ended abruptly once we began introducing different sections of the forms with headers. The form was broken up by standard titles “Donation amount” “Enter your information” “Credit card information” but we wondered if treating these steps in the process as a place to continue the conversation with the donor would help humanize the process and improve donor conversion on the page. We developed a treatment that guided visitors through each step on the page. We introduced the following treatment headers “Step 1: Would you like to make a one-time donation or support this work monthly?” “Step 2: How much would you like to give?” “Step 3: What is your billing information?” and “Step 4: What’s your payment information?” and split traffic 50/50 across a handful of donation forms.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that creating conversational headers on a donation page can result in a small increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for the overall traffic was not significant enough to be considered valid. A more significant learning is that the treatment had a significant impact on gender and specific visitor groups. Female visitors had a substantial increase in donations while other visitors had a significant decrease. This information can be used to inform future experiments by emphasizing conversational language for female visitors and potentially exploring other treatment options for other visitor groups. It is essential to note that further testing would need to be done to validate these findings and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How simplifying an appeal by removing specific impact equivalences from a donation form affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation dollar amount equivalencies on donation forms for donors will achieve a greater conversion rate because  it’s more direct to the point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 08/22/2022
This was tested on both desktop and mobile variants to determine if removing the presence of equivalencies would increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Overall donor conversion was lower for the variant (-2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (32%).
• Overall RPV was higher for the variant (10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (79%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Future tests will examine removing the equivalencies exclusively for mobile users and changing them to reflect recurring values instead of one-time.
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (31%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (16%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time RPV was higher for the variant (+10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the variant (9%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (66%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (50%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (93%).
• Mobile recurring RPV was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (86%).
• Mobile one-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (13%).
• Mobile one-time RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Desktop donor conversion was lower for the variant (-10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (84%).
• Desktop RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (60%).
• Desktop recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Desktop recurring RPV was lower for the variant (-1%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (51%).
• Desktop one-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-14%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Desktop one-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (59%).
• The data shows that removing equivalencies specifically favored recurring donor conversion on mobile devices.
• Recommendation is not to remove equivalencies on donation forms.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How a lower monthly ask impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by decreasing the monthly amount to $10 for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because the lower amount is more attainable and less of a financial sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 02/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, tv, digital efforts, and resources Pathway to Victory shares the truth of God’s word with people worldwide.
In January 2023, we emphasized Pathway to Victory’s Pathway Partner program. This is a recurring gift program where the donor makes a monthly or annual gift to PTV and receives resources each month and exclusive access to a library of sermons and other incentives.
Traditionally the entry Pathway Partner monthly amount has been $25. We decided to test an entry of $10 to see if this would increase the number of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall we only saw a 9.4% increase in conversions with a 47.2% level of confidence.
In revenue, we saw a small decrease in the average gift but that is not surprising due to the lower ask of the treatment.
The average PTV audience falls within the 65+ age range, however we saw that 35-44 visitors had a 415.9% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence (1 vs 5 donations). While this was not the purpose of this test, activating a younger audience, it is interesting to see that impact.
We will continue testing into how we can convert more people to become Pathway Partners.

experiments.title: How converting donation form into a multi-step form impacts mobile donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation form into smaller steps will increase donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2022 - 11/06/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They were wanting to test ways of increasing donor conversion, particularly for visitors using a mobile device. They hypothesized that the current form was too long for Mobile and caused visual friction and that a shorter form would lead to higher donor conversion for this traffic segment to the main donation page.
They ran the test for one month and found the following results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, we were unable to validate the impact of a 3-step form. There just wasn't enough of an impact and enough gifts during the test to validate. We will not roll out a 3-step form but will instead test other ways of removing friction and increasing the value proposition of the page. For example, adding additional value propositions around the form submission button or testing the impact of asking for credit card fees on the form.
We should also test in the future a step one of X feature so they know what step they are on.

experiments.title: How pre-populating form fields and re-ordering steps impacted donor conversion

rows.name: Prefilled Form Fields and Reordered Steps

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rearranging the order of selections and completing known form fields for instant donation page visitors will achieve a lift in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2022 - 04/09/2022
When evaluating how to increase donor conversion on instant donation pages for acquisition offers, we noticed that the control donation page asked first for the gift amount and then for the frequency of the gift. We wondered if placing the frequency before the gift amount would make more sense in beginning the conversation with the donors visiting the page. We also thought we could reduce the work involved in submitting the form by pulling in First Name, Last Name and Email Address from the previous landing page to help the donor continue filling out the rest of the fields. We launched the treatment with the pre-populated form fields and re-ordered steps across all acquisition offers and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we observed a 30% increase in donor conversion with 65% level of confidence, so the test results are statistically invalid. However, we did see a directional lift for mobile visitors, which had a 97.8% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence. Based on this learning, we can deduce that mobile visitors were more likely to complete the form when they had fewer fields to complete. Because the re-ordering of the gift options—putting frequency before the amount—decision making process did not appear to have a negative impact on transactions, we can continue to optimize the page with the frequency being the first choice for the donor to make by testing a tabbed form next. We can also use what we learned from this experiment in paid media efforts to also try this method on email appeals in the future since we can pre-populate personal information from the database in our email system and see if we get similar results.

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.

experiments.title: How making gift type the first decision point affects donor acquisition on premium offers
experiments.researchQuestion: Clarifying gift type at the beginning of the form will not harm donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2021 - 05/09/2021
FamilyLife found great success using tabbed donation forms on their main donation page. But before rolling this experience out to all of their donation pages, we wanted to ensure that this user experience did not not harm donor acquisition on acquisition offers. So we tested this on one of their most high performing premium offers, A Quiet Place. This was a 50/50 AB split test using an Optimize redirect. So half of the traffic saw the original format and the other half of traffic saw the tabbed form.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we were hoping, the tabbed form did not harm new donor conversion in any significant way. Neither revenue nor donations saw a valid decrease in this test. The next step is to roll this page out to their microsite.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
*This was the 2nd of an identical test to see if greater volume would impact significance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3.9%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching statistical significance.

This was the 2nd of an identical test after seeing an “invalid” result the first time. We thought that more volume could give a stronger indication of impact. The combined results of this experiment yield the same result…a 3.6% decrease, but with an invalid result.

If we determine that the benefit of getting this user-reported revenue data more valuable than the slight risk of small decrease in conversion, we’ll roll this treatment out.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate

rows.name: Progressive Form Field Asking for Organization Size

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2020 - 06/16/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching a significant level of confidence. It’s possible that running this for longer and getting more traffic to the page could prove that this decrease is in fact real – but the odds are low that this makes a significant impact over time.
We will continue to test the placement of this field, but knowing that the likelihood of a negative impact is very low. And the upside is that we get better segmentation and targeting for future outreach efforts.
